column1: Text

As the 20th century began, cars like the Model T Ford revolutionized travel. Now, a crucial question arises: are we overly reliant on automobiles? Reducing our dependence on cars is, in my opinion, essential.

Elizabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," underscores the importance of this. Cars dominate urban landscapes from Shanghai to Chicago. Consequently, experts find it challenging to mitigate pollution from vehicles. Shockingly, cars contribute to 12% of Europe's pollution, but this figure skyrockets to 50% in the U.S. due to our excessive car usage! Furthermore, Robert Duffer's piece, "Paris bans driving due to smog," vividly illustrates the environmental harm caused by cars, as Paris prohibited driving to combat smog.

Conversely, Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," offers a glimmer of hope. Millions of Colombians participated in a car-free day, opting for walking, biking, or public transportation, resulting in remarkably quiet streets. Annually, this event bans personal cars while allowing buses and taxis, significantly improving air quality. Additionally, Bogota has undergone substantial transformations, incorporating parks, recreational spaces, sidewalks, and commercial establishments.

Ultimately, diminishing car usage and promoting car-free initiatives are highly beneficial, leading to cleaner air and more pleasant cities. Bogota's car-free day demonstrates our capacity to reduce traffic congestion and enhance urban living. If we adopted similar measures in America, such as encouraging biking and walking for shorter journeys, we could foster a brighter future. In conclusion, advocating for reduced car use is both a judicious and commendable approach.

A solid transportation system is a must-have for any country; that’s why cars and trains are so essential for travel. Yet, all those cars create big messes, and everywhere we see pollution levels climb! The increased traffic is really ruining our air quality. Finding fast solutions to cut pollution is key.

Take the German town of Vauban, which is actually pretty neat. Not many residents there use cars. Since most shops and important locations are nearby, travel is easier. Although cars are still present, many Vauban residents don't even own one. Believe it or not, over half the families sold their cars upon moving there. They say it's less stressful since less driving is nice. These days, cars account for 12% of all pollution; however, in packed US cities, it might be as high as 50%.

So, Paris was really smoggy, so they tried something radical. No even-numbered license plates were allowed on Mondays. Enormous fines would be handed out if the rule was broken. The next day, it was the odd numbers' turn. Traffic decreased by 60% in just five days. Diesel cars, which are so popular in France, really worsened the smog. But, the ban wasn't permanent because they stopped the every-other-day thing.

Envision a car-free day like in Bogota, Colombia. One day a year, only taxis and buses are permitted. Pollution drops significantly on this day, and they've been doing it since the 90s. Later, they added bike paths, parks, and relaxation spots. Giving up cars might be difficult, but it helps the environment.

Look, we probably won’t get rid of cars completely soon, but countries need to try to reduce pollution and make driving less attractive. Taking action to combat air pollution will make life better. Even though we use cars a lot, too much driving is bad for us and the world.

Is America moving away from cars? Elisabeth Rosenthal poses an interesting question. It seems fewer people are driving, which is fantastic! Think about it: fewer crashes, healthier lives, and cleaner air simply because we're not driving as much. Do we even need cars the way we used to?

Firstly, driving less drastically improves health and safety, which is key, right? Andrew Selsky recalled the peaceful streets of Colombia on a car-free day. Fewer cars, therefore less stress! Traffic jams ruin our days and make us irritable. Thus, less driving, less traffic, less stress! Heidrun Walter felt happier and less stressed after selling her car. Moreover, we get more exercise. Cars are handy, but we forget to be active. Selsky noted numerous new parks and sports areas. Fewer cars could lead to more parks, inspiring us to move around. Essentially, less driving equals less stress and healthier living!

Furthermore, driving less benefits the earth. Although some may not care, it's truly vital. Pollution harms everything, and cars are a major source. Rosenthal states that driving less "will have beneficial implications for carbon emissions and the environment." Less driving, less pollution – a greater chance for the future. Pollution is a massive issue, and drivers worsen it.

Moreover, perhaps we overestimate our need for cars. Many believe they MUST own one, but are they addicted? Technology has altered us, and we neglect nature. Over-reliance on anything is unhealthy. David Goldberg believes "all our development since World War II has been centered on the car, and that will have to change." Some may disagree, but depending on something harmful isn't progress. Rosenthal notes that "cash-strapped Americans could not afford new cars, and the unemployed weren't going to work anyway." Not everyone can even buy a car. Those overly reliant on cars have forgotten nature and often disregard the environment. Driving less could assist with this.

In conclusion, less driving could improve health, reduce pollution, and lessen our dependence on cars. It's a cleaner, safer planet. Perhaps America is improving by driving less and considering the common good.

Do you ever notice how much you drive? To work, the store, everywhere! People use their cars all the time, even for short trips, which is crazy. We forget how much driving hurts the planet. Maybe skip the car today? "But I need my car!" people always say. But driving less is doable. Havana, Paris, Bogotá show us it can work. Less driving is good for you and the Earth!

Driving less might actually make you happier! Heidrun Walter said people are way less stressed when they don't drive. Think about how annoying traffic is. Finding your way, dealing with angry drivers – so stressful! Big cities like New York and Paris are known for bad traffic and pollution. Robert Duffer wrote about how Paris banned cars because of smog, explaining how hot air traps car fumes, which is really bad. He said the car ban quickly made the air cleaner! Instead of driving, you could walk, bike, or even rollerblade. These are healthier and make you happier than sitting in traffic.

If we drove less, cities would get way better. Pollution hurts plants, smells bad, and makes everything depressing. Less driving means less pollution and cleaner cities. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote that when there are fewer cars, more people walk, leading to more sidewalks and fun shops. Andrew Selsky talked about new parks, nice sidewalks, restaurants, and stores popping up. The EPA likes areas with fewer cars, Rosenthal said, because it cuts down on pollution. Bogotá, Colombia even has a "no car" day and fines drivers who break the rules. Fines are annoying, but they stop smog! Basically: less driving is great for you, makes cities better, and cleans up the planet.

Alright, cars are pretty cool, everyone loves them, but they're SUPER essential. I mean, we HAVE to have them to travel! Of course, we have public transport, but many of us would rather drive alone. But, many cars cause pollution and stress. If we all rode the bus or train, we would save money on repairs, have less traffic, and the air would be cleaner. So, driving less is a good idea.

Using cars less is good for us and the world, mostly 'cause the air improves. Cars make gross stuff. One car? Not a big deal. But MANY cars, like in Paris, are a real problem. Because of that, Paris BANNED cars 'cause of the bad air! Robert Duffer noted that cars with even-numbered plates couldn't drive for a time and got fined if they did, then it was the odd numbers' turn. That cut traffic by 60%! And Andrew Selsky says that Bogota, Colombia, has days with no cars to fight pollution, get people to try other ways to travel, and help the environment. All over, driving less is seen as a win!

Also, think about relaxing! Being stressed about traffic, car fixes, and all that noise makes you stressed. Less driving, less stress. No traffic jams, repair bills, or loud noises makes life easier. Elisabeth Rosenthal said Hartmut Walter told her, "I was always tense driving. Now I feel better.” That shows how not driving can ease your mind. Also, Andrew Selsky says car-free days "are a great way to relax..." Taking care of your head is key 'cause less stress makes life better.

So, driving less is a win-win for us and the earth. The world needs a break, and stress is bad. Driving less helps both. It might be hard to switch, but places are doing it and it works! It makes for a better, happier world.

Isn't the Electoral College totally weird? Instead of voting directly for the president, we pick people to vote for us, no matter if you are left or right leaning. But get this! Source two claims electors can just ignore our votes! Can that be true? We vote hoping to get something good in return, but this system is just chaotic. What makes it so bad? The winner gets everything!

We don't just vote for the president directly. Nope. According to source two, we pick electors. Each state gets a set number. After that, those electors vote. There are 538 electors overall. You'll need 270 to win the race. A state’s elector total is based on the number of Congress members. Shouldn't every single vote count? Let’s get rid of the Electoral College so every vote matters. Then, candidates have to work to earn our support, and ties? That would be rare. As it stands, if a bunch of people in your state wanted someone else, your vote just means nothing. Let’s ditch the college; your vote should matter!

Source two is also talking about "faithless" electors. Apparently, some might change their minds and vote for the other side. That's a huge betrayal! If a Republican votes Democrat, then all Republican votes just vanish. We do this every four years. Electors changing their minds? That’s not good.

According to sources two and three, there’s "winner-take-all." It's super rough. If you don't win, your votes are just gone. The person who wins grabs everything! Voters who picked someone who lost might as well have rooted for the winner.

Hey Senators, let’s get rid of the Electoral College! The system is broken. Some votes are just worth more than others. Electors can just do whatever they feel like doing. And "winner-take-all"? It just tosses out the votes of anyone who voted for someone who didn’t win.

Respected Senator,

I'm reaching out about a big change: how we pick our president. We should really think about getting rid of the Electoral College. We need a fairer system where everyone's vote counts right away! Right now, it's old and unfair; electors can go against what most people want – that's not okay!

Sure, changing the Electoral College won't be easy. But it's worth it in the long run. If we just counted everyone's votes, candidates would have to try to get support from everywhere. We'd also need to figure out what to do if there's a tie, to keep things fair. So, even if we can't totally get rid of the Electoral College, we have to fix what's wrong with it.

Right now, electors have way too much say in who wins. The "winner-takes-all" thing in the Electoral College makes voters feel like their votes don't matter. Candidates only spend time and money in a few "swing" states and ignore everyone else. Remember in 2000, when they basically ignored 17 states? Because of stuff like this, the Electoral College is old news and needs to be changed or scrapped.

Candidates pick electors on purpose to help them win. These electors get support without the candidate having to go to every state. Basically, the candidate just waits to see what happens. A candidate can win without even trying that hard, because the electors make the final call. This could lead to shady stuff like bribes in states that matter a lot – that's really scary.

When we vote, we're not actually voting for the candidate. We're voting for electors, which is weird. They get picked in different ways, like at state meetings or by the candidates themselves. One big problem is that you never know what might happen with the Electoral College. Back in 1960, some people in Louisiana even thought about replacing the Democratic electors with people who didn't like John F. Kennedy.

Basically, the Electoral College is a relic from the past. It gives electors too much power and messes with our democratic rights. Maybe it made sense a long time ago, but it doesn't fit with what democracy is all about today.

Okay, listen up! We're surrounded by cars, right? They seem simple to use. But, think about driving less. Seriously, imagine cleaner air and more money in your pocket. Less driving? It's a win-win!

Take Paris, for example. Robert Duffer said, "Paris bans driving due to smog." Their idea was pretty smart. One day, only cars with odd-numbered plates could drive. The next day, it was even numbers only. Break the rules, and you paid a fine! Plus, public transport was free for a week! Suddenly, traffic and smog? Gone! Air quality got better, and people saved money. Duffer pointed out a huge drop in traffic – about 60%! He said, "Traffic jams dropped 60 percent in Paris, after five days of smog so thick… it was almost as bad as Beijing, China, a city known for pollution." Even with terrible smog, fewer cars made a big difference.

Now, check out Vauban, Germany. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote, "In German Suburb, life goes on without cars." They went all the way, banning cars completely. About 70% of people don't own cars because parking outside town is super expensive, and cars aren't allowed inside anyway. People are happy! Heidrun Walter said, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Lots of young folks don't even bother getting a driver's license! Rosenthal explained in "The end of car culture" that cars are just for getting around. People aren't that attached to their cars; they just want easy ways to travel.

The main point is, cars are useful, but so are buses, trains, bikes, and carpools. And guess what? Some of those options are even free! Plus, less pollution and traffic makes life better for everyone, and for the planet too.

Plumer truly despises the Electoral College, with Source 2 labeling it as "ancient and dumb." A large consensus echoes this sentiment, decrying it as either a complete "disaster" or simply "stinky." While some defend it as a tie-breaker or a boon for larger states, these arguments often fall on deaf ears. The prevailing cry is for a straightforward popular vote, deemed superior without question.

The core issue lies in the unequal value of individual votes. Electors possess disproportionate influence compared to regular citizens. A direct vote would ensure that each person's voice carries equal weight. Moreover, electors are susceptible to bribery or undue influence, which is detrimental to democratic principles.

The Electoral College is widely considered an antiquated system. Plumer (Source 2) even cites Bob Dole's emphatic call to "Ditch the Electoral College!" This sentiment is shared by many who advocate for a direct vote, bypassing the Electoral College entirely. Some sarcastically suggest that a child throwing darts could select a more suitable president. Plumer (Source 2) notes that "Over 60% of voters want a direct election," a number that has only grown since 2000.

In essence, the Electoral College is inherently peculiar. It's a convoluted mechanism where electors, not the citizenry, determine the presidential outcome. Plumer (Source 2) elucidates, "You vote for electors, then they vote for the president. Who are these electors? Just normal folks. Who picks them? Depends on the state. Can we control them? Not always! Do people mess up? Sometimes!" One is left to question the true source of power.

While the Electoral College may offer certain advantages, such as preventing potential chaos, most perceive it as an unnecessary burden. A simple popular vote is generally regarded as the fairer approach. As Plumer (Source 2) concludes, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational!"

From the very beginning here in the United States, the Electoral College was written into the Constitution. It makes sense to keep it, simply because it has worked so far. Why mess with something that isn't broken? Sticking with the Electoral College seems smart. Here's why, in five quick points.

Okay, sure, there *are* some possible problems with the Electoral College. For example, the electors might just go rogue and vote how *they* want. Think about it: we aren't really voting for the president; we're voting for electors who then make the call. They could, in theory, ignore what the public wants and do their own thing. Then there is the chance that a state submits conflicting slates of electors; that is a complete mess waiting to happen. Plus, even though it's rare, the Electoral College *could* result in a tie. The "winner-takes-all" thing can also make candidates skip over some states where one party always wins. But with a direct vote, they'd probably try harder to get votes everywhere. Most of the worry, though, is about stuff that probably won't happen. So, maybe we should chill out and consider a different way of looking at things.

Voters in swing states? They're *way* more engaged because they know they decide who wins. They're glued to the news and know the candidates inside and out, because their votes really matter. Candidates spend a ton of time and money trying to win them over. But a lot of other states? They are pretty much set in stone because one party is super popular there. Maybe voters in those swing states are more focused on what they actually want and believe. Maybe they are the perfect folks to pick our president.

The Electoral College keeps us from needing runoffs, which makes everything smoother. A super close direct vote could cause huge problems without it. Just think about Nixon's win in '68 and Clinton's in '92. The Electoral College really showed its value; they each won with only about 43% of the vote.

I get why some voters in states that always vote the same way might not like the Electoral College. It makes sense that they might just skip voting altogether since their votes don't seem to matter. One vote doesn't count for much on its own, and lots of people just didn't bother voting in 2012. Some people just want to say what they believe, even if it doesn't change anything. If you're involved in the government, though, your vote has a way better chance of actually making a difference.

Senator,

Hear me out - scrapping the Electoral College? Real bad idea! We got 538 electors to wrangle, ya see? Bagging over 270 of their votes? That's the golden ticket! States pick these guys, usually loyal to one candidate. Keeping this system is crucial. How come? Easy - it makes picking a winner crystal clear, plain and simple!

Why stick with it? Well, mostly because the Electoral College gives us a clear winner, pronto, without all the extra drama. Could it tie? Sure, maybe, but that's rare, even as things are. Remember 2012? Obama snagged almost 62% of electoral votes, but won only 51% of the popular vote. Most states? Winner-takes-all! A tiny win in a state = a boatload of electoral votes! Sure, it makes candidates obsess over swing states, ignoring others, maybe. But usually, the winner lands way more electoral votes than the people's votes.

And, the Prez needs to speak for *everyone*. So, the Electoral College makes candidates hunt for votes far and wide. No single place has enough votes to crown the president alone. Even wildly popular somewhere, you still gotta hit the road. More votes where you're already loved? Doesn't get you more electoral votes. A president nobody backs? Recipe for total chaos! People feel worthless.

Bottom line: Keep the Electoral College! It nixes ties and makes sure the president serves *all* of us.

Heidrun Walter used to be anti-car, but now she loves them! She feels super free. Lately, everyone's ditching their cars. We all thought we needed them, but sales have tanked, and hardly anyone's driving. Turns out, driving less is actually pretty sweet. It's a sure bet to help curb climate change, cut down on pollution, and boost the economy for everyone, not just those big auto companies.

Car fumes are terrible for the greenhouse effect, which is awful for us and the planet. One study says that cars cause a whopping 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases and almost half in some American cities! Less driving equals less pollution, which is fantastic for our earth. It helps tackle major problems, like global warming and rising sea levels. In essence, fewer cars, fewer greenhouse gases!

Smog and pollution are the worst. They damage our health and our world. The article said, "After days of near-record pollution, the city banned some cars to clean up the air." The air got way better fast! Doing this more often could seriously reduce pollution. It might even get people to try buses, bikes, or walking! One study showed that banning cars can create new bike paths, parks, sports centers, better sidewalks, and less traffic. Basically, less driving, better city life!

Finally, less driving helps the economy in a bunch of ways. One source said that in the US, we spend a fortune on roads. If we didn't need cars as much, that cash could go to schools and other important things. Plus, less driving saves people money, so they can spend it on other things. One source even thinks that car ownership trends suggest a big shift. There's been a massive drop in the number of young adults getting licenses. This lets us really focus on growing the economy. So, less driving equals more government money, more savings for everyone, and economic growth.

Basically, using cars less is awesome. It helps the economy, combats the greenhouse effect, and reduces pollution in cities.

Picking a president? Super important! The choices they make? Big results, good or bad. The Electoral College is *supposed* to show what each state wants, but does it REALLY work? I think it does a pretty good job showing what each state thinks and what the country feels overall.

People complain about the Electoral College. Some say it doesn't always pick the best person or that it's just not fair. But hey, we get to CHOOSE! What we think matters, and every single vote is worth a LOT—way more than not voting at all. It's not perfect, but what is? Overall, I believe the Electoral College does a decent job counting votes.

Coming up with a better way is HARD. One worry is that the Electoral College might not match the popular vote. But I don't think that's true. Our votes help the Electoral College pick who we want, and I like how it gets states to work together by showing how each state feels.

Okay, some folks argue that big states have too much power in the Electoral College. Maybe that's a little true, but it makes sense, right? States with more people should have more votes. Even so, each vote counts the same, which keeps the big states from taking over. Even when we disagree, the Electoral College helps us understand what the country is thinking, which is a good thing.

In the end, the Electoral College picks each state's favorite by using most of the votes to pick a winner. It has problems, sure, but just adding up all the votes might be worse. Every system has issues, but the Electoral College gives us a pretty organized way to count votes over the years. You can't please everyone, that's for sure!

Esteemed Senator, I've been racking my brain about the optimal method for picking our President and their team, and an idea struck me. Essentially, we're staring at two paths: the Electoral College versus a straightforward count of every citizen's vote. Weighing both, one decidedly emerges as the superior choice, without a doubt.

The Electoral College relies on electors – specific folks – to cast votes for the President and Vice President. These 538 individuals hold the ultimate power to declare the winner. Frankly, I'm not thrilled with this setup. We think we're directly electing the president, but really, we're just picking folks who then make the big decision. Why should a tiny group, when stacked against millions of voters, wield such influence in choosing our leader? We're left biting our nails, waiting to see what they decide. It feels downright unjust that their views carry so much more weight than everyday people when it comes to running our country.

Alternatively, a popular vote lets each person directly support their presidential candidate and running mate. This way, we clearly signal our desired leader, hoping for good things. Even if a candidate loses, your vote still counts; it builds the national voice. Sure, the Electoral College may offer a quicker answer, but the understanding that your vote truly has significance could ignite bigger participation and excitement in the candidates and what they stand for. We seize the power to mold our future, instead of just crossing our fingers that the electors agree with us.

Admittedly, the Electoral College has some possible upsides, such as guaranteeing a definite victor. Some might even say picking electors is basically the same as voting directly. However, these small perks fade when you consider giving every citizen the right to a direct vote, which, if you ask me, is what democracy is all about.

In the end, when thinking about how we elect the president, the main question boils down to: How loud do you want your voice to be? Do you want to be hands-on in the process, or are you okay with letting others make the call? For me, it's an easy one: I'm all in on the system that strengthens our democracy and ensures everyone gets to speak their mind.

Imagine a nice family walk in the park, but uh oh, there's nasty pollution! It's super clear we use cars way too much. How about trying the bus sometimes, or maybe walking, or even sharing rides? Seriously, driving less is a big win! Cars cost a ton, make it harder to use other ways to get around, and hurt the Earth.

Having a car can really empty your bank account! In Vauban, Germany, a parking spot can cost a crazy $40,000 – more than some houses! And if you drive on the wrong day in Bogota, Columbia, get ready to pay $25. Driving in Paris? You might have to pay 22 euros just because of your license plate! And don't forget gas is always getting more expensive! Rich folks might not care, but families with kids sure do!

It's easy to forget how great public transport is. Picture no buses, trains, or subways. Now, try driving into New York City; good luck with that! Bogota gets people to use buses or walk by fining drivers who break car rules. Mayor Antanas Mockus was super happy that lots of people joined in, even when it rained! Cities like Cali and Valledupar did the same, which got people talking. Even New York has bikes to rent. Anyway, public transport is key to saving the planet.

Oh my gosh, sometimes the smog is so thick you can't see the sky. Paris stopped cars for a bit because there was too much pollution, and the air got 60% better in just five days! But France likes diesel fuel, which is really bad for pollution, so it's tricky. Bogota's car-free day is a nice break from smog, and Vauban barely has any smog because not many people drive there! Even small changes can really help our Earth.

Vauban and Bogota prove that less driving means less pollution. Paris bans cars to fight smog. Vauban's crazy parking costs make people not want to drive. Bogota fines drivers to get them to walk or take the bus. We need to drive less because it saves money, helps other ways to get around, and cleans up the air.

Reducing car use is great for our town! Other countries, like France and Colombia, are coming up with fresh ways to get around. It's much more chill if we don't always drive. Research even shows fewer locals have cars these days. This is a real opportunity to test out cool ideas, just like in Vauban or Bogota, so we don't waste our lives in traffic.

Think about this: A town in Germany shows that life without cars is possible. Elisabeth Rosenthal mentioned that Vauban, Germany, is largely "car-free." Most folks there prefer public transport. The article also said, "70% of families don't have cars, and many sell them when they move there!" One person said they feel calmer and happier without a car because they avoid driving stress. Many places want to be like this, cutting back on cars to help the planet. Let's hope for more "smart planning" down the road. If we try to drive less, our town could become a car-free paradise.

Bogota, Colombia, has car-free days, so people can try different ways to travel and clean up the air. Visitors often say how much better the air feels. Going "car-free" gets people moving and makes the city look better. Reports talk about more parks and sports fields, wide, clean sidewalks, and cool shops and restaurants. It’s not only about cleaner air; it makes the city a nicer, more appealing place to live!

Picture using ideas like Bogota and Vauban. Fewer locals are buying cars already. Some are even skipping driver's ed. One writer noticed Americans aren't as into cars anymore. Another pointed out that we drove the most miles in the past, and it's been dropping since then. Young people are a big part of this change. They use social media, phones, carpools, and buses more, which reduces their driving. Car companies might not like it, but many of us believe we'll have better and easier travel options in the future because of this.

Basically, less driving means less pollution and stress, nicer neighborhoods, more exercise, and saving resources. Cities everywhere are trying to cut down on cars to help the environment. If we go green and car-free, we'll join a global trend!

Dear Senator, I am writing to ask you to get rid of the Electoral College. I really believe that who becomes president should only be decided by the total number of votes from all over the country. To be honest, I think the Electoral College is really unfair and could be bad for what the U.S. Constitution is all about.

The Electoral College messes up the main ideas of our democracy. Even though we vote, the Electoral College can go against what most people want, which isn't right! This makes you wonder if our democracy is real. Think about the 2000 election: More people voted for Gore, but Bush won because of the Electoral College. Like "The Indefensible Electoral College" says, "More than 60% of voters want an election that's easy to understand, not this confusing thing." It's clear that people want a simple way to pick their leader.

Also, I really don't like the Electoral College because it might hurt the U.S. Constitution, especially our basic rights. The most important thing is that we, the people, get to choose our president. But when the person with the most votes loses because of the Electoral College, it hurts democracy. "The Indefensible Electoral College" worries about possible election problems, asking: "What if a state sends in two different lists of who the electors are? This happened in Hawaii in '60. Luckily, Nixon agreed to the electors picked for his opponent." Can you imagine how crazy things would be if that happened again and the wrong person won! The Electoral College is just asking for trouble.

To sum it up, let's get rid of the Electoral College! It's just not fair and could really mess up our U.S. Constitution. I really hope you'll think about what I've said when you're making decisions about this important issue.

Everyone worries about how transportation affects the environment. But, often, we don't think about how much better things would be if we drove less. Cars constantly pollute. So, think hard about what it means for the environment before you buy a car. The harm to the planet might be worse than the convenience of driving.

Think about all the cars, buses, and trucks we see every day! The amount of fuel they use is a huge problem for the environment. The stuff that's released when fuel burns hurts the planet badly. If we don't change, the ozone layer could disappear and Earth could become unlivable! Plus, lots of people drive way too much when they could easily walk or bike. In Paris, when the air was really bad, they stopped people from driving as much. Guess what? Traffic went down by a massive 60% in less than a week! Our everyday driving habits can really mess up the environment.

Owning a car costs way more than you think. In Vauban, Germany, if you want a car, you have to buy a parking spot for almost $40,000 – and that's on top of what you pay for your house! Gas is super expensive, and insurance makes it even worse. On top of all that, driving can be scary, stressful, and just plain annoying.

Vehicles are ubiquitous! Their prevalence profoundly shapes our existence. But consider a car-free scenario! The planet would surely benefit! Excessive car use degrades air quality, an unwelcome consequence for all.

Bogota, Colombia, instituted a "No Car Day," an initiative ripe for replication! Instead of driving, citizens opted for walking, cycling, or buses. The outcome? A palpably cleaner city! Despite minor rainfall, participation remained high. The mayor dismissed weather concerns, prioritizing cleaner air through alternative transit. Contrast this with the U.S., where car dependence poses a formidable barrier to such measures!

Paris combatted vehicular smog with driving bans during pollution peaks. Initial resistance was strong, with Reuters reporting nearly 4,000 tickets and 27 tows! Yet, traffic plummeted by approximately 60%! Despite this, smog persisted, akin to Beijing's struggles. The long-term viability of this approach remains uncertain.

Reduced driving may inconvenience some due to increased transit challenges. HOWEVER, emerging studies reveal declining rates of car ownership, usage, and licensing among Americans. A Drexel University professor suggests a shift. Forced driving cessation may deter a return to prior habits, potentially resulting in sustained lower car usage in the U.S., a boon for air quality.

Global environmental degradation stems from vehicular emissions. While reducing driving presents difficulties, the advantages are clear! Envision cleaner air and increased pedestrian, cyclist, and skater activity. Altering our relationship with cars? A decidedly positive environmental step! Reduced driving? A veritable act of planetary preservation!

If you're hoping to cut down on driving, your location and personal investment really play a big role. All over the world, cities are experimenting with reduced car usage, even aiming for a *complete* absence of cars. Heavily polluted areas are *especially* motivated to remove cars from the streets, striving for cleaner air for their residents.

Rosenthal's piece, "In German Suburb...", vividly illustrates the essence of a car-free lifestyle. When done right, walking and biking become remarkably attractive options. And *this* is crucial. Given the issue of pollution, it's certainly worthwhile to explore implementing similar approaches in other locations.

The pollution in some cities is truly awful; the conditions are dire. In Duffer's "Paris bans driving...", the air quality in Paris plummeted to such alarming levels that a driving ban was enforced! People were, understandably, upset. Violating the ban resulted in fines, and potentially even vehicle confiscation! Duffer points out that "Congestion was down 60 percent...", highlighting the ban's dramatic impact on reducing traffic.

Around the globe, initiatives are being developed to facilitate pollution-free transportation. Selsky's "Carfree day..." details Bogota's remarkable success. Millions abandoned their cars and opted for walking, biking, or skating – even when it rained! Selsky observes that Bogota now feels *better*. Improvements to "Parks…sidewalks" have enhanced the overall cityscape.

The world is changing at a rapid pace! Young people are starting to question, "Do I *actually* need a car?" They're leaning towards more affordable and environmentally friendly alternatives. The concept of *needing* a car is gradually fading, though it hasn't vanished entirely.

The Electoral College, a hot topic! Should it stay or go? Is it just plain odd? We think we vote for a president, but we're really choosing someone to choose the president. If America's about the people's will, shouldn't the person with the most votes simply win?

What *are* we doing when we vote for president? Are we just picking someone to vote later? Really? This article says we're choosing "your candidate's electors." Okay, but if the Constitution says we vote *directly* for president, how does *that* work? It seems backwards, no? And can we *trust* those electors to do what we want? Like the article says, can we *really* control them? Not always! Could your person lose even if most people in the state voted for them? Is the Electoral College stealing our voice, making our votes meaningless? Honestly, it's confusing, and our voice *should* matter.

Some say the Electoral College makes sure a president gets elected, but does it guarantee the *right* person wins? It often feels like our votes don't count, like something's being taken away. The popular vote should be king! Remember 2000? Gore won the *most* votes but lost! The article mentions a poll from back then, saying most people would rather vote directly. If our votes *really* mean something, let's vote on whether to keep this thing around!

People claim the Electoral College is efficient and stops fraud, but is that *really* true? Every system has issues, but is this one a *good* solution? America's all about listening to the people's votes. We should vote directly, no middleman.

In short, the Electoral College is unfair, broken, and against American ideals. It stops us from directly choosing who we want, making us pick electors instead. If you believe in American ideals, you should see how wrong this is. It's a bad way to run elections, and we need *real* control!

Senator, respectfully,

The Electoral College – why is it still here? Some say it’s bad for democracy because we vote for electors, not the candidate directly. But, hear me out – it *actually* functions well! It swiftly selects a winner and gives us a president for everyone.

It’s designed for a decisive win. A simple popular vote? Chaos! Richard A. Posner notes that victors receive many more Electoral College votes. This reduces debates about who truly won. He's spot on, making it simpler! Also, no ties like the near one of Nixon vs. Clinton in '92. The Electoral College takes more into account, ensuring a clear decision.

It also pushes the president to consider all citizens. Candidates require votes nationwide, not just in friendly areas. We need a president for all of us! Imagine Republicans only focusing on the South—unfair! Bradford Plumer claims candidates ignore states they expect to lose. Perhaps those states are already set! Why would a Democrat bother with Texas, a Republican stronghold? Or a Republican in California, a Democratic state? Voters there are likely decided! Yet, Plumer’s ideas might be outdated. Things evolve! Dislike the Electoral College? Look at current data. Review recent elections! Public opinions in 2004, 2008, and 2012 hinged on events, pledges, and youth voter turnout. The Electoral College makes the president mindful of the whole country.

Therefore, keep the Electoral College! It identifies the winner and guarantees the president represents everyone. Electoral College vote numbers? Larger, making the win obvious. It also requires the president to think about the entire nation, not just certain regions. Senator, please support it! It has worked well and should continue to do so.

Back when the world was young, getting from point A to point B meant one thing: walking. Groceries, a bite to eat, or a visit to Granny – your own two feet were the go-to mode. So, what sparked our car craze? Today, it's all about cities wanting fewer cars hogging their roads.

Cars aren't exactly Mother Earth's best friend. But hey, there's hope! New rules are popping up, all about curbing our car habit. Take Vauban, Germany: folks are ditching cars for the planet, and they're loving life! Heidrun Walter, a Vauban mom, said, "Cars gave me killer headaches. Now, I feel amazing!"

Paris, that famous city we all know, slammed the brakes on cars because the air was just plain awful. Guess what? Traffic took a nosedive – a whopping 60 percent drop – in just five days!

Since Paris was choking on Europe's worst air, they had to pull out all the stops. The car ban worked wonders, cleaning up the air and kicking smog to the curb.

Big apples like New York? Streets packed with cars. With buildings so close together, walking or biking is often quicker than driving around. Plus, New York's got cool bike lanes. And carpooling's a hit, with people sharing rides to cut down on cars. That means less pollution, fewer traffic jams, and saving some cash on gas.

Bill Ford, Ford's big boss, imagines a future where owning a car is a pain. He wants to team up with phone companies to build cities where "people walking, bikes, cars, trucks, and buses all get along, saving time and energy, cutting pollution, and boosting safety." Sure, cars come in handy, but banning them everywhere? Not gonna happen. Still, using them less is a win. Too many cars? Too much pollution! What if we had a car-free day to try out other ways to get around? Biking rocks – great exercise, faster than walking, and keeps you fit while getting you there sooner!

So, presidential elections, right? What a wild ride, especially with that Electoral College thing! Keep it or toss it? It's like our votes mixed with what Congress wants. Those past elections? What a mess! And who knows what's next! Shouldn't we, in a democracy, just pick the president ourselves? Seriously, we gotta fix this, and maybe scrap the Electoral College, like, yesterday.

Now, here's where it gets weird. Instead of voting straight for the president, we pick these electors who vote for us. Then, even when the most people vote for someone, the "winner-take-all" thing messes it up. "The Indefensible Electoral College" says it's bad for voters. Candidates play favorites, skip states they can't win, and obsess over those key swing states. Some states get all the love, others get ignored. Like in 2000 – seventeen states? No visits at all! Seriously?

And remember the "disaster" risk! Bradford Plumer thinks we got lucky in 2000, but the system could break. Picture Louisiana trying to switch Democratic electors to mess with Kennedy. It can get that crazy! Or, states send different lists of electors, or we tie. Even with small tweaks, we need a way better plan ASAP.

Richard A. Posner defends the Electoral College, saying it keeps things steady and helps small states against the big ones. But that's not fair; it helps small states more. Posner says voters in swing states get more attention. Plumer disagrees, noting places like Rhode Island and South Carolina were ignored. Lots of voters in big TV areas didn't even see ads. Simple: the Electoral College gets messed with too easy and needs to go.

Seriously, I'm still saying, "Ditch the Electoral College!" People should vote directly for their president. Hard fix, I know, but the current way? So unfair! Some might defend it, but please! We need to redo the whole thing, or at least change it big time.

Dear Florida Senator, The Electoral College is creating arguments! Some folks love it. They say it picks our presidents very well. Others want changes due to problems. We need to update it for today. Shouldn't our system show what people really want in a true democracy?

Fans say Electoral College ties almost never happen. Electors usually vote as people want. But even if rare, such issues are serious! Small issues grow big, like a hole in a sock. Let's fix it fast to avoid bigger problems! Electors usually follow votes, but someone might go against the people!

Does the Electoral College steal your vote's impact? You vote, but electors pick the winner. Some states get more electors, which is strange. Some votes matter more, which feels wrong! People don't vote, thinking their voice does not matter. Candidates focus on swing states, and other voters feel lost.

Besides voter apathy, the Electoral College has big flaws. Ties are rare but can happen! The House then decides, maybe against what the nation wants. Also, someone can win the vote but lose the election. Remember Al Gore in 2000? This is a broken system and needs fixing! We must fix it before more issues arise.

The Electoral College looks okay, but it does not show what the people want. Electors decide, not us. This is a problem, especially when we claim to be a government by the people. Surely, there's a better way to show democracy. Do the right thing! End the Electoral College and support a fair system that shows what America truly stands for.

Alright, here goes nothing! Hopefully, this works out!

While cars get you there fast, all that pollution they make really messes up the environment. Simply driving less makes a big difference. How so? Less pollution, less smog, and we save stuff too!

First, when you drive less, greenhouse gases drop right away. Instead, ride a bike, hop on a train, or just walk to work! Cars make nasty exhaust that turns into gases that hurt the world. One study (we'll call it Source 1) says regular cars make around HALF of those gases! So, fewer cars equal a happier world, plain and simple.

Plus, not driving really cuts down on smog. Smog is seriously awful stuff that harms both the environment and you! Imagine air that's super gross and makes you feel sick. Lots of cars can make smog super bad in some places. Source 2 said Paris's air was once almost as bad as China’s and everyone knows China's air is terrible. If we drive less, there's cleaner air! Paris cut smog by 60% in just five days by just cutting car use! Clean air is definitely a win!

Finally, less driving saves resources! Source 1 mentions that if we don’t use cars as much, cities won’t need to get so big. Less land's used for roads, stores, and homes! Source 4 says that this saves tons of building materials and protects natural resources. Less driving makes everything better.

Basically, driving less is great for the Earth, great for you, and saves resources for the future.

A lot of people don't like the Electoral College, that's for sure. But it does have advantages, no kidding! The Constitution set it up this way for a purpose. The idea? To keep Congress or the average person from having too much power in choosing the president. It's like a safeguard, really. It makes sure all Americans get a say, honest! State leaders get a real chance to support what their citizens want. Sure, people complain, but it's still a good way to pick the President, agreed?

Those running for president? The Electoral College keeps them busy! They can't only focus on one area, like the West Coast, and expect to win. No way! They must win over people who might not agree with them at first. That's fantastic! A President who appeals to many people will likely do a better job for all. Plus, the focus on swing states? It gets people excited, keeps them informed, and gets them ready to choose wisely who to vote for. Seriously, it does!

In fact, keeping the Electoral College makes sense. How come? It usually picks the person most people vote for! Okay, in 2000, Gore got more total votes, but Bush won the presidency because of the Electoral College. Crazy, right? But it rarely happens! Before that, you'd have to go way back to 1888! Political parties pick Electoral College voters, and they almost always vote for their party's candidate, as they should. Getting rid of the Electoral College and using only the popular vote? Could cause issues. What about a tie? Yikes! Unlikely, yes, but what if? The Electoral College helps when there is no clear winner.

Some say the Electoral College makes people skip voting in states where their candidate has no chance, like Democrats in Texas. But every vote matters. It still counts for how a state's electoral votes are given out. People vote in presidential elections to show support, not just to make the final decision. The Electoral College has worked for a long time and will probably keep working well.

Want to save Earth? Drive less, alright? European car emissions make up about 12% of greenhouse gasses, per studies. Quitting cars is hard, but there are choices! Carpool, bum a ride, bus it, train it, cab it, or bike! Act now to save the planet!

Cars hurt our air! Some places charge to drive less. Paris had an idea. After bad smog, they briefly banned cars! This shows change is doable! Beijing was like Paris in smog. Now, the government cleaned it up. Bogota bans 7.2 million from driving on a "Day Without Cars." Break the rule, pay $25 or go to jail. Millions of Colombians "walked, biked, skated, or took buses," said Andrew Selsky, who thinks "This plan might spread." Parks thrive, and the air is cleaner.

Driving less helps you! Clean air, less traffic. Walking cuts heart issues and obesity! Car companies may hate this. Sales could fall, hurting Ford, Toyota, Honda, etc. But folks will still drive. Less car production might boost the economy by lowering gas costs. Get this: Driving is down each year! Experts cheer for Earth, while car companies sweat.

Get the hint? Cut car use! Young people dislike cars. Youth driving dropped 23% from 2001 to 2009, a 2013 study says. Falling sales worry about the U.S. reaching "peak driving," but experts rejoice. Now what? Good or bad? The future’s a mystery! Obama’s plans to cut gases seem to work. Studies show that "Americans buy fewer cars, drive less and get fewer licenses each year."

About the Electoral College, Senator, wouldn't a simple popular vote be a better way to decide things? A huge amount of people don't even vote – almost half! "In Defense of the Electoral College" section 23 mentions people feeling their vote is useless. Politicians spend all their time in only a few states!

Senator, according to "In Defense of the Electoral College" section 23, in 2012, "only half of eligible Americans voted." Think about it – why bother if your candidate can't possibly win? Also, "The Indefensible Electoral College" Section 11 tells us the Electoral College has been messed with before! Segregationists even tried messing with Democratic Electors to hurt Kennedy.

But hold on! Some want to keep it, saying the Founding Fathers thought it up as a compromise for fairness, giving everyone a voice, per "What is the Electoral College" Section 1. "In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 15 suggests it might be good because people can sway electors, and hey, history matters! "In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 16 even says Electors should vote for who actually won.

Senator, this winner-take-all thing is just not fair! It's all about the big states. All the time and money goes to them. "What is wrong with the Electoral College" Section 13 points out that "Seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all, including Rhode Island and South Carolina, and voters in 25 of the largest media markets didn't see a single campaign ad!"

Senator, what about the popular vote? Wouldn't it be great? People would actually vote if they knew it mattered directly. Power to the people, right?

It feels like cars rule the world, doesn't it? It seems no one walks or bikes; they just drive everywhere now. This shift reshapes everything, wouldn't you agree? Is this really our vision? Rosenthal, Duffer, and Selsky understand this issue. They each desire fewer cars, but for distinct reasons. Their research highlights that fewer cars bring more leisure, boost business, conserve resources, and diminish our "car culture."

Do traffic jams constantly make you tardy? Perhaps walking or biking could be faster! Selsky shared insights on Colombia's car-free day. Millions chose to "walk, bike, skate, or ride buses," leading to roads surprisingly "free of traffic jams." Less traffic equals less waiting around! Walking benefits the environment and also saves time. Businesses flourish with fewer cars. Selsky points to "new restaurants and fancy shopping areas" emerging, alongside "wide, smooth sidewalks." More walking translates to more shopping opportunities!

Conserving resources emerges as a major perk of reduced driving. Rosenthal notes that cars contribute to "12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe... and even up to 50 percent in heavily car-dependent zones." That's huge! We *absolutely must* drive less as car emissions harm our planet. Some cities now enforce "no-driving" days. Duffer noted Paris initiated a "partial driving ban" after "days of near-record pollution." Reduced driving equates to less pollution and reduced traffic! Excellent.

The reign of car culture may be waning. Studies indicate young Americans drive considerably less these days. Rosenthal cites a "23 percent" decrease in young drivers. Maybe it's due to tickets, parental influence, or environmental awareness. Rosenthal suggests experts believe this will lower "carbon emissions and help the environment." Consequently, car companies are adapting, and technology is reshaping our commutes.

To sum it up: less driving brings more free time, stronger businesses, resource conservation, and a diminished car culture. Next time, consider driving less. It's beneficial for our planet.

Okay, so, the Electoral College? It’s got to stay. Sure, it's old, but picking a President this way is still easiest. Counting every single vote makes elections way harder. Recounts? Fewer of 'em! And it keeps the big states from calling all the shots, which is good. It keeps things from getting totally out of hand.

The Electoral College helps us avoid those messy fights. You often see candidates getting way more electoral votes than actual votes. Take Obama, for example. He snagged almost 62% of electoral votes, even though just about 51% of folks voted for him. States use "winner-take-all," making figuring out electoral votes easy, even if someone barely wins. A tie? Yeah, possible, but super unlikely. But counting every single vote? That's just asking for chaos.

With the Electoral College, we don't have to do runoff elections. These happen when nobody gets over half the votes. Nixon in '68 and Clinton in '92? They only got around 43% of the popular vote. Still, they won big, with 301 and 370 votes respectively. So we get a clear winner without having to vote again. And nobody wants to waste time with runoff votes.

So, the Electoral College? It stops those big states from being too powerful. We gotta keep it around. Maybe there's something better out there, but, for now, it’s the best system we've got.

*Click!* Mom just snapped a pic of my face with her iPhone. Holding that little gadget felt like I had superpowers. Ah, youth! I can still picture myself sprawled on the kitchen floor, pushing a toy car while Mom cooked; the garlic smell was everywhere. And then, finally, my driver's license! A huge deal for us teens, right? But let's be real, it’s not all sunshine and roses. Less driving means less pollution, which means everyone gets to breathe easier.

Now that I'm driving, I can't stand the smog that makes me squint on my way to school. Ugh, and my car's making it even worse! Car fumes create smog, and that sends greenhouse gasses into the air. Shockingly, U.S. cars are responsible for around half of those emissions (Source 1). And get this—even making gasoline pollutes! It's such a waste, releasing tons of carbon. So, the fuel-making process emits greenhouse gasses, and THEN our cars pump out even MORE?! AP Environmental Science hammered home that too many greenhouse gasses are a NO-GO. They trap sunlight and UV rays, leading to global warming. Plus, smog is just plain nasty. Paris was choking on it, so they banned cars to clear the air. And guess what? In just one car-free day, the smog vanished! People were breathing freely again (Source 2). Smog also hurts Paris' tourism—who wants to see Versailles covered in pollution?

Driving less does wonders for our bodies and minds. Take Vauban, Germany, where cars aren't allowed. Loads of people who moved there ditched their cars and are now happier! Heidrun Walker, a media trainer and mom of two, said, "When I had a car, I was stressed. Now, I'm happier." (Source 1). Walker's story shows that saying goodbye to your car can seriously boost your mental well-being. And your body reaps the rewards too! People are walking, running, biking, or skating all over. Bogota, Colombia, has a "Day Without Cars" every year, encouraging people to walk (Source 3). Walking is so great for your heart! If more places had car-free days, maybe we'd see fewer heart problems.

So, less driving equals cleaner air, healthier bodies, and happier minds. Next time you're reaching for your keys to drive to your friend's place, just pause for a sec. Think about your health, the planet, and consider walking instead.

Elections? Totally vital! Not an everyday occurrence, ya know? Your vote? Seriously impactful. It can reshape things for the nation. Selecting the president *should* be a cinch: tally the votes, presto, you've got a winner! Simply vote for your top pick. Piece of cake. The popular vote rocks, ditch the Electoral College weirdness.

Many folks think the popular vote's more just. Super straightforward: vote, count, most wins! Candidates try hitting up various states and chatting with people, time permitting. Yet, Bradford Plumer claims the Electoral College makes candidates "skip states they can't win, focusing only on tight races." How unfair! Every state deserves candidate visits. Some candidates ignore whole states! Plumer noted that "in the 2000 election, seventeen states, like Rhode Island and South Carolina, were ignored. People in 25 big cities saw no campaign ads." How can voters decide if candidates don't talk to them?

Also, the popular vote's simpler than the confusing Electoral College. Popular vote? Super simple: vote, count, most wins. Electoral College? Plumer says if there's a tie, "the House decides, with each state voting for president. The Senate picks the VP. Each state gets one vote, so Wyoming's vote, even with 500,000 people, equals California's 55 reps, representing 35 million voters." Imagine the chaos! One vote having such power! The popular vote is clear: everyone votes, the most wins. No need for reps to complicate things.

Basically, the popular vote rocks 'cause it's fair. We pick who we want, no meddling. Plus, it's easier than the Electoral College. If tied, reps might vote, maybe clueless. Voting's super important, so let's get it right. We should vote our way, not let others, especially reps, decide. Voting really counts!

Alright, so the Electoral College, how *exactly* does it work? The Federal Register states this: We vote for *electors*. Then, these *individuals* meet and select the President and Vice President. After this, Congress counts *all* the votes. Essentially, we are choosing people to choose the *President*. It seems quite simple, right? What's the big deal?

Many people want to abolish the Electoral College and simply use the popular vote system. Bradford Plumer questions whether electors will even vote as we *expect* them to. And he doubts it! Perhaps sometimes, but we still pick electors and *hope* they choose correctly. We *must* trust them. However, if we voted *directly* for the President, we would *still* have to trust that they would be *competent*. It is basically the same, but with an *additional step* included, don't you think?

Richard A. Posner makes a *compelling* point: "Why eliminate something we *already* disapprove of?" He believes the Electoral College maintains fairness, similar to how the Senate aids small states. *Precisely*! Large states have little influence in the Senate. Thus, with the popular vote, large states gain *even MORE* attention because *that* is where the majority of people reside.

Additionally, the Electoral College helps stabilize things if no one receives more than 50% of the votes. Like in '68! Nixon received the *most* votes (43%), but not a majority. Clinton had a similar situation in '92. However, they won the Electoral College, so there was no runoff. That was *close indeed*!

I support the Electoral College. It prevents Texas from dominating Rhode Island. Moreover, it helps us avoid runoffs. Some argue it is outdated, but I believe the Electoral College is a *reliable* voting method.

This Electoral College system? It's really quite terrible - antiquated, inherently unfair, and, frankly, absurd! Proponents offer justifications, but these arguments lack logical coherence. Shouldn't individual votes count directly? Even Dole understood its obsolescence! Those supposed benefits? They're illusory. And trust the electors? Don't bet on it! They pledge loyalty, yet their actions remain unpredictable! Consider Nixon, Carter, Dole, Chamber, even the AFL-CIO – all recognized its flaws and desired its abolition. The public believes they're choosing a president, but it's actually electors! What if a deadlock emerges? The House resolves it, but with skewed representation!

These electors? Direct presidential voting is not their actual role. Confidence in them? Unwarranted! Can voters select unsuitable electors? Absolutely! So, the Electoral College is flawed, with vulnerabilities to manipulation. Voters presume they're electing a president, but surprise! It's more complex. We don't even choose *electors* directly! Loyalty should be presumed? Not so fast! In '60, Louisiana nearly diverted electors to undermine Kennedy. Shocking! Easy manipulation, shaky foundation. "Faithless" electors have betrayed trust. Recurrence is likely. The system? Rigged! Abolish it! Empower the people!

Should electoral votes deadlock, the House determines both president and vice president. That's even worse! Profoundly unjust. A travesty of democracy! Each state receives a single vote. Wyoming, with minimal population and one representative, wields the same influence as populous California! Does that even make sense? Of course not! Yet, such is the reality. Split-ticket voting leads to divided government. Consequently, the House might impose an unwanted leader. Deadlocks uncommon? Think again: minor vote shifts in '68 and '76 could have triggered them. Small changes, significant outcomes. "Every vote counts?" A deceptive platitude. This system? Its continued existence is unbelievable. Equal representation is a right.

Enough: abolish the Electoral College. Direct democracy is the answer. Straightforward! Electors are untrustworthy. The House is unjust. Let people vote! Give us the right to vote! A President "For the people" ought to be selected "By the people!"

Listen up! Driving causes major issues for the planet. Is it possible to reduce the amount we drive? The world is really suffering, and urban areas are becoming severely contaminated. Automobiles are polluting everywhere, from here to Europe, even as far as Central America; this is a worldwide problem. Consuming so much gasoline is fundamentally destroying our planet. But, what if we reduced the amount we drove, even slightly?

Okay, so Paragraph 10 of that article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," opens with "After days of near-record pollution..." Paris, dealing with smog? That is terrible! The "City of Love" can barely breathe! If Paris is dealing with these warnings, how do other major cities handle things? We have more of everything: manufacturing plants, vehicles, anything you can think of! What exactly is our course of action? If we do not act, it is only going to worsen. We must act now!

Paragraph seven of "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," contains David Goldberg's quote: "All of our development since World War II has been centered on the car..." Why do automobiles take precedence over human well-being? I understand vehicles are faster, but what sacrifices are we making to accommodate that? Forget gasoline; what is happening to our health? Our resources are allocated to roadways rather than community parks, and there are shopping centers instead of grassy spots. Trees are effective for clean air, but pollution is wiping them out. Smog? A long time ago, no one even recognized this; but now, it is all due to vehicles!

So, driving less is crucial, though we can accomplish this. What about specific days where driving is not permitted? Drive, get fined! They do it in Bogota, Colombia. The passage even declares, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota." Maybe this can be applied to us, too! Even Cali and Valledupar are trying it out.

I visited California last summer, hoping for a good time. You know, surfing, mountains—the whole California vibe. I got to LA, dreaming of a vibrant place like Miami. Wrong! Seriously, the smog and crazy traffic wrecked it. Maybe it's normal, but fewer cars would be a game changer. Cleaner air and safer roads? That's a big score!

Elizabeth Rosenthal pointed out, "Cars are behind 12% of Europe's dirty air... even 50% in certain spots" (Rosenthal, paragraph 5). And trucks pile on too. With pollution so clear, many want less traffic. Places like France, Colombia, and even the US are doing something. And guess what? It's helping! Rosenthal mentioned, "Surveys reveal fewer Americans are buying cars, driving less, and getting fewer licenses" (paragraph 29). Cars will stick around, but less use is a plus.

Think about less traffic in cities like NYC; less crashes would happen. The US has around 500 crashes a day, and globally it's over 5000! Car wrecks kill many, but we can end this. Sure, reckless drivers don't help. But consider: less crashes means less pollution. Duffer stated, "Traffic fell 60 percent in France's capital" (Duffer, paragraph 14). Plus, Bogota's Car Free Day inspires Latin America to cut traffic and get people walking.

People now see why less traffic rocks. Less driving means better air and less accidents; that’s what really matters. I left the city and the freeway, and I finally saw the real California!

Alright, so what exactly is making the air so nasty? Primarily, automobiles are a significant contributor. However, reducing our reliance on them is absolutely fantastic! Just consider the positives: improved respiratory health, money saved, and enhanced safety on our streets for everybody. Feeling motivated to give it a shot?

Without a doubt, a high volume of vehicles exponentially worsens pollution levels. Remember that time when Paris restricted car usage during a severe smog event? The immediate result was pristine air quality. Furthermore, decreased driving negatively impacts the profitability of major oil corporations. Instead, ponder alternatives such as bicycles, pedestrian travel, public buses, skateboards, or trains – infinitely more appealing choices!

Furthermore, prepare for your bank account to experience substantial growth! Traffic violations can result in considerable fines. Furthermore, vehicle ownership, including expenses like fuel, maintenance, and penalties, can be exceptionally costly. Decreasing your driving habits translates to considerable long-term savings.

Finally, contemplate the matter of security. One study indicated that altering our transportation methods, by incorporating biking, pedestrianism, and public transportation, enhances safety. It's quite straightforward: fewer cars equate to fewer collisions. Additionally, reduced vehicular traffic creates a more secure environment for cyclists, scooter riders, and pedestrians. Not to mention, fewer intoxicated drivers!

Essentially, abandoning car dependency constitutes a mutually beneficial scenario: cleaner air, greater financial resources, and more secure roadways. So, what incentive might compel you to forgo driving on occasion?

It seems most folks think cars are a must-have. Sure, they get us around, but they're a real problem for the Earth. And somehow, they make walking or bus rides seem lame. But what if we just drove less? Something to mull over! Other countries are already on it, so we can do it too. Fewer cars could mean a healthier planet, nicer places to live, and way better public transit.

Cars are seriously messing up our world. Check out Elisabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars." It's about Vauban, Germany, and their fight to cut down on those nasty greenhouse gases. The article says cars cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gas emissions. And in the US, where driving is a big deal, it's even worse, like 50%! That's a load of pollution, making cities icky. Driving less could really lower those numbers. Governments are also stepping up. For example, Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," tells about Paris, France, banning cars because the smog was so bad. "After days of crazy pollution, Paris only let certain cars drive to clean things up. People with even-numbered plates had to stay home or pay 22 euros. The next day, it was the odd-numbered plates' turn." And guess what? "Traffic in Paris dropped by 60% after just five days of smog!" Even small changes in our driving can make a big difference.

Fewer cars also make cities way nicer. Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," talks about how Bogota, Colombia, got better by ditching some cars. "The car-free day is part of a plan that started in Bogota in the mid-90s. They built lots of bike paths, the most in Latin America. Parks and sports areas popped up everywhere. They even fixed those terrible sidewalks, making them nice and smooth. Rush-hour traffic is much better, and new restaurants and shops opened up." Cities are now thinking about people on foot, realizing cars can be a pain and trying to make things better for everyone. In "The End of Car Culture," Elisabeth Rosenthal discusses the US trying to drive less. Bill Ford, the head of Ford Motor Company, wants to team up with phone companies to build cities where "walking, biking, cars, buses, and trains all work together to save time, gas, reduce pollution, and keep everyone safe." Lots of folks are trying to make public spaces better for getting around in an eco-friendly way.

More and more, people are getting that we don't need cars as much, so they're driving less. In "The End of Car Culture," Elisabeth Rosenthal mentions fewer people are getting licenses and buying cars. "America's love for cars is fading. The number of miles people drove peaked in 2005 and has been dropping since, according to Doug Short from Advisor Perspectives." The article also points out you don't need to own a car all the time. "The Internet lets you work from home and hang out online. Cities are becoming more attractive, so people are moving back from the suburbs. Phones and carpool apps make it easier to get around, like shared vans for commuting. So, people who stopped driving because of the recession might not start again." Now, it's super easy to find a car when you need it, so you don't have to own one all the time.

Driving less is a really smart move. With fewer cars, we can tackle big problems like pollution. Cities are changing to make public spaces better for everyone. Cutting back on our car use is a big deal for making people's work, time, money, and lives better, both here and around the world.

Look, as someone young, the Electoral College feels ancient. Though I can't vote yet, my thoughts matter too, right? I believe it should stay. If we chose the President solely by popular vote, it'd be a huge mess quickly. Certain regions might adore a candidate, but the rest might dislike them entirely. That'd be a complete disaster.

Choosing a leader only by most votes could be a really awful idea. Article three, paragraph 20, the "winner-take-all" rule, forces candidates to focus on key states. Because these states decide the election, people there are well-informed. However, with just a popular vote, anyone over 18 could vote for dumb reasons. Young folks might back whoever their friends like, or just because they like the party. People might not consider their choices deeply, but the Electoral College stops that.

Also, a popular vote might elect someone known only in one part of the country. Article three, paragraph 19, says a President popular in just one area wouldn't be a good leader, as one area can't elect someone alone. Imagine if popularity was all that mattered. Like, Rodney is super popular in the South, but since he doesn't need more electoral votes, he doesn’t need to visit the South. If that’s all it was, voters elsewhere might feel ignored, and the President wouldn't care about them. He’d still not be “their president,” even if he won. The Electoral College fixes this. We vote for electors who then vote for the president. Winning the most votes in a state means its electors support them; that's fair. A popular vote unfairly hurts people who didn't vote for the winner.

I know some disagree. Article two says, "Candidates don’t visit states they can’t win... Seventeen states didn't even see the candidates in 2000!" Candidates skip states they don’t think they can win. I see the case for the popular vote, where candidates would visit every state. But, I disagree. A candidate visiting all 50 states doesn't guarantee they'll win. The Electoral College makes sure a state's majority matters, not just overall popularity. It’s like high school: the popular kid might be a jerk, while some less popular kids are nice. Choosing a President based on popularity could ruin our country.

Mr. State Senator, the Electoral College matters a lot. A popular vote could give us a bad president or one who wins just because they are popular in one area. Please consider my thoughts when you discuss this.

Envision a world greatly improved, with dramatically fewer cars choking our streets. Just ponder the enhanced health and safety our world could experience!

Seriously, what if traffic vanished? The atmosphere would be so much cleaner! Wouldn't easier breathing be a boon to all?

While some may bristle at the notion, diminished driving truly benefits us collectively over time. Picture inhaling polluted air constantly – a nightmare for your lungs, right? Furthermore, reduced driving means fewer gas station stops. Saving money is always a win, isn't it? You're free to allocate those savings however you wish!

The hidden costs of cars are staggering! Insurance and weekly gas expenses add up quickly. Even with currently low gas prices, a sudden spike could severely impact your finances!

Therefore, is incessant driving truly worth the environmental and financial toll?

Wouldn't life be more peaceful if cars didn't dictate our every move?

The final decision rests with you, though!

Do you even consider the pollution spewing from your vehicle? Probably not! Your focus is likely fixed on your destination or your phone, not the environment. This makes sense because environmental issues often appear distant. We typically only become aware through alarming news reports.

Sadly, because the harmful impacts of car pollution accumulate gradually, the devastation to our planet goes unnoticed.

Continuing this way, both plant life and humanity will struggle to breathe, potentially requiring hospital treatment!

Imagine a life without the burden of car payments or the perpetual need for fuel.

Countless individuals and families face financial stress, grappling with bills and gas expenses just to commute to work.

Instead of your car constantly guzzling fuel, why not opt for walking or biking on occasion? Consider the potential cost savings!

Perhaps scrapping the Electoral College isn't the greatest idea, no? The Founding Fathers created it so both Congress and regular folks could chime in. Now, folks grumble, claiming it's unfair because the electors are the real decision-makers, not us. We simply vote for them. But electors flipping their votes? Extremely rare, it's just chatter. So, despite the gripes, the Electoral College should remain. It simplifies matters, reduces vote counts, and amplifies the power of smaller states.

Alright, the Electoral College can be a bit of a mess, like in the year 2000, but it doesn't happen nearly as often as arguing about who scored the most votes overall. The reality is, a person can snag the Electoral College victory without winning the popular vote. Take 2012: Obama snagged 61.7% of the electoral vote but only 51.3% of the popular vote. When a candidate wins big in a state, even a narrow win translates into something huge.

Moreover, the Electoral College prevents elections where no one secures over half the votes. Consider 1968 and 1992, when the victors only held around 43% of the vote. That could lead to stalemates and endless voting! Yet, the Electoral College just picks someone right away. Some even think it discourages folks from not voting in states where their candidate is bound to lose, such as Democrats in Texas or Republicans in California. If it's futile, why bother showing up at all?

Despite all this, the Electoral College levels the playing field, offering smaller states a chance against the big boys. In 2012, Florida was neck and neck, and its 29 votes went to Obama. Candidates put in extra effort in larger states because they carry more weight in the Electoral College. People moan about the winner-takes-all situation, but candidates zero in on swing states rather than squandering time where the winner is already a foregone conclusion.

Sure, some might skip voting, figuring their vote doesn't hold much sway, but one vote almost never determines an election's outcome. People vote to voice their beliefs, not because they think they're the sole reason someone wins. The Electoral College, not just the popular vote, should still select our president; plain and simple.

The Electoral College, right? I'm just not getting it. Even though Richard A. Posner wrote a book trying to justify it, I still disagree. Remember Florida in 2012? Obama won all the electoral votes with just 62% of the vote, so what about the 48% who didn't vote for him? Their votes basically didn't matter! It’s a winner-takes-all system, no matter how close it is. Bradford Plumer understands—only swing states are important. In 2000, candidates ignored seventeen states. Only the swing states got attention. If we had a popular vote, wouldn't everyone matter? Each vote would be equal, no matter where you live in the US.

The Electoral College could create big problems. Plumer mentions that electors can vote however they want. It's rare, but it's possible! Just one person could cause chaos. And what if it's a tie? Each state gets one vote? That's ridiculous! People in large states have the same voting power as people in small states? A popular vote would solve all of this.
