Posner attempts to defend the Electoral College, but his reasoning is weak. He thinks a popular vote would lead to more disputes? Where is the evidence? He claims candidates would only visit their home states? Maybe, but they'd likely lose. He says the Electoral College educates voters in swing states? Seriously? A popular vote would educate *everyone*. He thinks it benefits large states? No way. Candidates will go where the most voters are, it's obvious. Runoff elections? Okay, his point is somewhat valid. They could be inconvenient. However, it's still better than being unfair or risking a voting catastrophe!

Defending the Electoral College seems nearly impossible. So much evidence shows that it’s unfair and risky. A national popular vote? We need it now! If you support the Electoral College? You're supporting inequality and a potential crisis. Surely, no one wants that!

The U.S. is known for enjoying life, going after what you want, and making sure everyone gets a fair chance, yeah? But this Electoral College deal? Seriously? These electors, meant to vote how their state wants, are actually the ones who choose the president. Is that really how a democracy should work? People are still arguing about it. Lots of folks think it’s a bad system, and you can see why.

Imagine this: You vote like you mean it. Someone gets picked to vote for your choice. Then – boom! – they change their mind and vote the other way! Now you're stuck with someone you hate. Is that fair? Suddenly, those electors seem shady. Wouldn't it be easier to just count up all the votes? The person with the most wins, plain and simple. No need for all this complicated stuff. And get this: The person who got the most votes has LOST because of the Electoral College, leaving the person you liked in the dust. Voting should be easy. We fought for it.

Besides just those questionable electors, the Electoral College has other problems. Sure, ties might not happen often, but elections have been super close. Some say the Electoral College makes things easier when there's a tie, but does it really? If everyone just votes honestly, fixing ties should be easy. One elector changing their vote can change the outcome for thousands of people. Also, electors often get all the attention when their candidate wins, even if bigger things in the country or state are the real reason.

So, the Electoral College makes you wonder about fairness and trust. Regular folks don't have much say over electors, and the system can lead to ties that aren't real. Since the U.S. is all about equality, a simple, easy-to-see voting system just makes more sense.

Do you ever wonder about the impact cars have on our planet? Think about it: dirty air and resources that are disappearing quickly! Can you imagine a future where we need masks just to breathe because of all the smog? But if we choose "green" options and drive less, we can help our communities and the Earth! Imagine cleaner air, fun parks, and cool ways to travel. Seriously, go green and get ready to be wowed!

Ever think about cars and the gross smog they create, especially in cities? It's awful! Car fumes get stuck, making it hard to breathe. Paris even stopped cars once because the smog was super bad, right? They banned cars on some days, and the air got much cleaner! One time, Paris's smog was almost as bad as Beijing, China! But guess what? After the smog left, cars came back, and so did the pollution!

Cool towns often have fewer cars! Take Vauban, a town in Germany where you don't need a car. Check this out: about 70% of people there don't have cars, and around 57% sold their cars to live there! Heidrum Walter, who lives in Vauban, said, "Having a car was always annoying. Now, I feel way better." Also, Bogota, Colombia, wants to reduce cars too! These car-free lives are great for us and the planet!

Okay, okay, cars probably won't disappear right away, but the good news is that there are lots of other ways to travel now. In NYC, renting bikes is super common! Also, carpooling is getting bigger to make roads less packed! Interestingly, recent numbers show fewer young people in the US are getting licenses and buying cars. Imagine a future with fewer cars!

So, driving less is a major win for the Earth. It can really cut down on smog, and the Earth will be much greener because of it!

According to Goldberg, part of Transportation for America, roads after WWII became mainly for cars, and he wants new ideas. The situation is evolving because cars are less essential now than they used to be. Ever wondered why less people are buying cars?

Things are changing fast, like in Germany. An example is Vauban, a "smart planning" area from 2006. It was made to exclude cars. Now, remarkably, more people in the suburbs are walking, cycling, or using trams to get to their jobs or schools. "Smart planning" can really assist towns in reducing driving. In Vauban, 70% of families don't own a car, and they're happier and less stressed from driving. Believe it or not, 57% actually sold their cars to move there! These car-free communities are appearing more frequently, making cities and suburbs denser while decreasing travel.

A major reason is that we have to deal with global warming immediately. Cars produce a large number of greenhouse gases. They account for about 12% worldwide and an incredible 50% in car-dependent countries, such as the US.

Paris experienced horrible smog. How did they fix it? They prohibited cars with even-numbered plates, punishing anyone who disobeyed, which effectively decreased traffic. Cheap diesel caused this smog. Since it worked well, Paris later banned odd-numbered plates because the capital was still smoggy.

Cars have dramatically altered our daily lives. But studies in the US are showing something important: individuals are buying fewer vehicles, driving less, and fewer are even trying to get licenses. Despite Americans' fondness for cars, many are wanting a greener world and seeing the true advantages of living car-free. Consider the trouble with insurance, monthly payments, never-ending repairs, safety issues, and the stress of driving. Sure, cars are helpful, but their drawbacks are more obvious. Fewer teens and young adults are getting licenses, and a growing number are choosing to avoid car ownership entirely. Why even own a car?

People everywhere are carefully assessing the good and bad of owning a car. They realize the importance of driving less, which will ultimately change the way they live. Slowly, one of history’s most groundbreaking creations is starting to matter a little less.

Our planet is changing fast! People are really trying to cut down on greenhouse gasses. One big thing we can do is drive less, or even ditch cars completely. Sounds weird? Well, some places are proving it works, and it's paying off! They are seriously reducing pollution from cars.

Check out Vauban, Germany, near Freiburg. It has no parking spots, driveways, or garages! Instead, people hop on a tram to get to Freiburg. Heidrun Walter, a media teacher with two kids, loves it. Vauban, done in 2006, shows a growing trend in Europe and the US: living in the suburbs without always needing a car. This little area lets its 5,500 people easily walk to shops on the main street.

Europe makes about 12% of the world's greenhouse gasses, but the US makes a whopping 50%! Take Paris, for example. The smog got so bad, pollution was crazy. The city stopped some cars from driving. One day, cars with even-numbered plates couldn't drive. The next day, it was odd numbers. If you broke the rule, you got a fine, but the city offered free public transport all week.

Bogota, Colombia, even has a "Day Without Cars" to get people to drive less. Millions of Colombians found other ways to get to work. This city of seven million asked people to try other ways to travel to fight smog. Mayor Antanas Mockus said that even rain didn't stop people. That year, Cali and Valledupar, two more Colombian cities, joined in. Enrique Rera, the mayor of Asuncin, Paraguay, said, "These people are starting something big, and others are jumping on board!" Besides the "Day Without Cars," Bogota has rush-hour rules, better sidewalks, and new places to eat and shop.

Vauban's car-free design, Paris's short car ban, and Bogota's car-free day show how we can drive less. People want to make changes to lower greenhouse gasses. Car sales in the US are going down, and fewer young people are getting licenses – a 23% drop from 2001 to 2009! The goal? Save resources, lower greenhouse gasses, and make things safer. With cities helping out, it looks like we can do it!

So, you wanna cut back on driving? Awesome! Initially, it reduces pollution when you do so. Truthfully, automobiles generate significant pollution! Several reports show cars contributing anywhere from 12% to a whopping 50%! To be blunt, less driving helps the Earth breathe, which is obviously healthier for us all.

Consider this: Skipping the drive to the office can reduce your stress levels! The daily commute to work or school can exhaust anyone. Simply contemplating traffic congestion can induce stress. Interestingly, traffic flow has improved in some locales, owing to minor alterations during peak hours. Therefore, experimenting with alternative modes of transportation, or minimizing driving distances, can inject tranquility into our hectic existences.

It is notable that young folks are realizing the benefits of driving less. Here's a surprise: the number of young licensed drivers is declining. Research indicates approximately a 25% reduction in young drivers nowadays. The upshot? Quite possibly, fewer accidents and heightened safety for everyone, especially the young ones!

In a nutshell, numerous benefits arise from choosing to drive less. From decreased pollution and stress to more secure conditions for youth, embracing alternative transportation methods instead of driving fosters more pleasant and eco-friendly communities.

Alright, check it. There's a big shift happening. Politicians and everyday folks alike are thinking about ditching the car more often now. For example, get this: there's a town in Germany that's practically car-free. And guess what? People there are seriously happy! But it's not just Germany that wants cleaner air. Even Paris, France is playing around with the "no-car" idea. Sometimes, when the air gets too gross, Paris just says "no cars allowed." The crazy part? In just a week, the air quality shoots up! Both Vauban (Germany) and Paris are trying out different ways to cut down on car use. But hold on, Colombia has *three* cities doing the same thing! Every year, they have a day where cars are a no-go.

So, what's the deal with Vauban in Germany building a city with barely any cars or parking spots? Think about it: most suburbs have houses super spread out, no stores nearby, and a ton of cars. Basically, most suburbs are cookie-cutter, right? But all those cars are a pollution machine. Vauban fixed this by making driving a pain. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote an article called "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars." The article highlights that cars are responsible for about 12% of Europe's greenhouse gasses. But, in some U.S. areas, it's closer to 50%! Vauban helps clean up the air by prioritizing walkways and shops over roads, making it easier to get around on foot. The money saved from skipping road construction goes to other transportation options. Rosenthal's article mentions that typically, 80% of transportation funds go to highways. Only 20% is left for everything else.

Paris is really pushing for a change in how people use cars. There's so much to see in the city, and honestly, you can get almost anywhere on a bike. So, why pump more pollution into the city by driving? That's why Paris sometimes puts a temporary stop to cars. The pollution in Paris is nearly as bad as in Beijing, China (which everyone knows is polluted). Robert Duffer wrote "Paris Bans Driving Due To Smog." Originally, Paris considered a complete car ban to kill off pollution. But, that wasn't going to fly because so many people rely on cars. So, they found a middle ground. On certain days, cars with license plates ending in an even number were off-limits. The next day, it was the turn of cars with odd-numbered plates. This went on for a whole week. Drivers who broke the rules had to cough up 22 euros. Duffer's article pointed out that nearly 4,000 drivers paid the fine that week! Even with the fines, there was a 60% drop in traffic!

Bogota, Colombia, also puts limits on driving. It's a city with almost seven million people living there. Every year, they have a car-free day. People walk, bike, skate, and hop on buses. Andrew Selsky wrote "CarFree Day Is Spinning Into A Big Hit In Bogota." The article explains that the city wants to cut down on pollution, traffic, and accidents by encouraging people to use taxis, buses, and other ways to get around. After three years of success, Cali and Valledupar (two other cities in Colombia) jumped on board. Selsky says the car-free day was part of a plan to make Bogota a better place, starting back in the mid-1990s. As a result of this plan, they built 118 miles of bike paths! Plus, new parks and sports facilities.

Even in the U.S. (where we love our cars), things might be changing. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote "The End Of Car Culture." She mentions that in the past 8 years, fewer young people are bothering to get their licenses and drive. Instead, they're walking, biking, taking the bus, or using ride-sharing services. With traffic getting worse and worse, these options are often faster than driving. And when people do drive, they're carpooling more often. This all helps to reduce air pollution.

A great many individuals express disapproval of the Electoral College system. As early as the year 2000, a Gallup poll revealed a preference among the majority for a straightforward popular vote mechanism. Nevertheless, it is imperative to consider the multifaceted nature of this issue. The Electoral College may once have been advantageous, but surely the current landscape has shifted considerably?

Selecting electors through the Electoral College can prove to be an arduous and protracted process. Initially, states are tasked with identifying their electors, followed by the planning and execution of subsequent gatherings. On Election Day, determining eligibility and navigating the intricacies of the process can induce confusion. These electors subsequently cast their votes for the President and Vice President, after which the votes are tallied. Given the 538 members constituting the Electoral College, a minimum of 270 votes is requisite to secure the presidency. A direct election approach could streamline this process substantially.

The implementation of a direct election would bring about transformative changes. Every vote would carry weight, potentially fostering greater unity among the states, as individuals yearn for a sense of significance. Given the unpopularity of the Electoral College, many perceive a direct vote as a superior method for electing the President and Vice President.

Within each state, each candidate maintains their own roster of electors, typically chosen by their respective party. Discontentment with these electors may lead individuals to perceive their vote as inconsequential, thereby diminishing their perceived influence on the outcome of the presidential election.

In essence, widespread discontent with the Electoral College prevails, owing to its perceived complexity and time-consuming nature. Suspicions arise that officials perpetuate its existence to safeguard their own positions. A direct election has the potential to rectify this situation and facilitate a more streamlined process for selecting our nation's leader.

Since time immemorial, humanity has strived for a more simplified life. Early humans engineered tools for hunting and fashioned rudimentary carts for transport. A significant nuisance of that era was horse manure! It was a constant task to keep the streets clean of it. The advent of cars seemed promising, but alas, they brought with them a different problem: smog.

Recently, Paris decided to ban certain vehicles to reduce the city's severe air pollution. Unexpectedly, this decision also affected Paris's local climate. Nighttime temperatures fell noticeably, while daytime temperatures rose. Some attribute this to the presence of dirty diesel vehicles, but realistically, the combustion of any fuel source generates pollution. Consider Vauban, Germany, where private vehicles are strictly prohibited! Inhabitants opt for alternative transport because the bus and rail network is excellent. The residents of Vauban report feeling less stressed than car owners. Children enjoy the freedom to play outdoors safely, which means they might be spending a healthy amount of time away from screens. Is Vauban too extreme? Think about Bogota, Colombia, where once a year, citizens observe a car-free day. On this day, only taxis and buses are permitted to circulate, and those caught violating this rule face hefty fines. Even within the United States, a noticeable decline in driving among young people is observed. Between 2003 and 2009, driving mileage decreased sharply by 23%. As superior alternatives emerge, reliance on personal vehicles wanes. How long has this been going on for? By the spring of 2013, driving rates had dipped to levels mirroring those of 1995, when far fewer families owned cars. Believe it or not, New York City even encourages and supports the organization of carpools!

Unfortunately, numerous regions such as Florida lack adequate public transportation infrastructure. Attempting to commute from Homestead to Miami presents a substantial challenge! The journey entails driving to a local bus stop, transferring to a bus to reach the nearest train station, enduring an hour-long train journey, and then boarding another bus for the final thirty-minute leg. That's three hours lost EACH way. The equivalent of a staggering thirty hours per week! By contrast, driving takes a far more reasonable hour, making it the preferred option for most. Even running local errands from Homestead to downtown Miami or Doral can consume the entire day. Without access to a personal vehicle, one must sacrifice ENTIRE DAYS or commit to excessively early wake-up times.

Looking closely at Vauban, residents seem generally more content than those constantly navigating traffic. Pedestrians are more inclined to engage in spontaneous conversations during their strolls. The air quality is superior, a stark contrast to the smog-filled air of congested metropolitan areas. It's estimated that cars account for around 12% of all pollution across Europe, however, this figure exceeds 50% in the United States! Had Paris implemented their restrictions sooner, the city might have seen a reduction in air pollution sooner. In the decades following the Second World War, massive investments were channeled into automobile infrastructure. Envision the possibilities had those same resources been allocated to bolstering medical advancements or alternative fields. In the U.S., the majority of transportation funds go toward highways rather than buses and trains. Vauban and Bogota thrive because businesses and housing are located in close proximity, and the bus routes are well-designed. While completely phasing out cars may be unrealistic given our current dependency, we can certainly make progress incrementally.

Listen up! The country's air is getting pretty bad, you know? A simple fix is to drive less often. Seriously, it can really cut down on smog, since vehicles cause a lot of pollution. You could hang out with your pals more often and help improve the neighborhood.

Cars make tons of smog. The less you drive, the less pollution there is. If there's less car pollution, the climate can chill out. That means everyone can breathe a whole lot easier and stay healthy. In car-free areas, the air is already fresher. Even "Car-Free Day" in Columbia proves my point! And less exhaust means less smog. You can prevent crazy pollution in busy cities if you drive less.

Here's the deal: When you cut back on driving, you have more time to relax. Think about walking, chatting with neighbors, or biking to the park. In places like Vauban where there are no cars, people feel less stressed because there's no traffic. It's interesting that younger people aren't as excited about cars as older people. Now they would rather take the bus or share rides so they can kick it with their families and friends.

When people drive all the time, there is no money left to fix sidewalks and roads for biking. Less driving? Easy fix! The pathways are fixed quicker than you can say "road trip." On "Car-Free Day" in Columbia, people were excited to build new sidewalks and over a hundred miles of bike lanes. They also built shop spots, fields, and parks. If you drive less, towns will be cleaner and have more fun places. Plus, it can reduce car accidents and make streets safer.

Think about driving less, alright? This can improve everything. The community will be stronger, everyone will be healthier, and the air will be cleaner.

Trading in our everyday car rides for different choices? It’s a fantastic idea! Think about much less traffic! Smog-filled cities like L.A. and Beijing could definitely breathe better. Also, drivers might have less stress, and more funds—who dislikes extra money?

Smog creates serious problems, particularly in crowded cities. L.A. nearly smells like smog, and Beijing is frequently covered in it. Automobiles greatly contribute to smog. Europe's vehicles create 15% of the air pollution; however, in the U.S., it's a staggering 50%! Fewer cars suggest less pollution. Need a car? Try hybrids, such as a Prius. Some cities, like Bogota, plan "Car-Free Days." Locals enjoy them, and other cities are attempting these days since they really reduce air pollution. During a severe smog issue in Paris, officials banned cars (excluding hybrids and carpools). This shows how severe smog can become.

Driving can be very bothersome. Traffic jams always destroy schedules and irritate drivers. Motorists become angry when someone cuts in line, and they worry about accidents. Imagine removing that anxiety! Vauban, Germany, eliminated most cars to improve living conditions. Residents possess vehicles; however, park in a costly garage beyond town (at nearly $40,000!). Individuals can reach locations by walking—shops, restaurants, everything. Cars mainly serve extended journeys. Walking to collect mail or experiencing nature sans cars lowers worry. No dodging traffic to eat! With smartphones and internet access, staying in contact without driving is simple. Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram enable communication, eliminating unnecessary drives.

Finally, less driving results in greater savings. In areas such as car-lite Vauban, you might not require a vehicle. A quality car costs $30-40k. How would you spend that wealth? A dream house, a memorable vacation, or a child's education. Since everything's easily reachable via walking, it seems excellent, isn't it?

Think about scrapping the electoral college. Just giving the win to whoever gets the most votes sounds risky. The electoral college, while confusing, really helps us pick a leader. It's not perfect, for sure, but it might be better than the other option.

Okay, a popular vote has some good points. Richard A. Posner writes, in "In Defense of the Electoral College," that it's the electors, not us, who vote ("source 3 a15"). But think: a real popular vote puts power directly with individuals! Are we even familiar with the electors? Bradford Plumer says, in "The Indefensible Electoral College," that state parties pick these people, so voters don't know much ("source 2 a 10"). That's not exactly fair, is it?

Even if voters are lost, electors usually do what they're supposed to. Plus, the electoral college gives us a clear winner every time. No ties here! Posner says, "The winner always has more electoral votes than popular votes," turning close races into big wins ("source 3 a 15"). See how it works?

Also, the electoral college makes candidates talk to all sorts of voters. Posner explains, "One area can't decide everything, so the president needs to appeal to everyone" ("source 3 a 19"). To win, you need to win over many groups. Swing states, especially, matter a lot and have smart voters. Posner thinks "People in swing states have power and know what's going on" ("source 3 a 20"). Candidates better pay attention!

A popular vote might sound good, but the electoral college makes the election more organized. It gives us stability and forces candidates to meet regular folks by going to different places.

Alright, the Electoral College, right? It's always talked about when elections come around! There are 538 votes total, and you need 270 to win. States with more folks have more votes. A big state like California? They get 55 votes, while little Rhode Island only gets 3. Remember way back in 2000? Al Gore got more popular votes than George W. Bush, but he still lost! That's when folks began questioning the Electoral College for real. Some want it gone, but it's still super important, I think. It's major in how we pick our president. It makes sure the winner wins big, picks a president for everyone, respects voters in swing states, and stops any ties from happening.

Without it, picking a president would be a total disaster. The Electoral College is there so we always know who won fair and square. If there's a tie, the Senate sorts it out. Each senator gets a vote, which is why even smaller states get a say. Plus, candidates have to talk to lots of different areas of the country. The president has to represent everyone, not just a few. That's why swing states are as important as they are. Winning states like Florida, New York, Texas, and California can change an election like that! These states have a ton of people who really care about voting. In swing states, people pay attention to what candidates are actually saying. The Electoral College keeps elections from happening where no one gets over half the votes. Like when Bill Clinton won with only 43% of the vote and still had 370 electoral votes. It stops us from having to vote again. So, we vote for electors, which some folks don't like, but it makes sure someone wins fair, which is what counts.

So yeah, let's keep the Electoral College! It's got 538 votes, you need 270 to win, and it's got rules to stop any ties. That's why we get a clear winner every time, it picks a president for everyone, respects all the voters in swing states, and stops elections from ending in a deadlock.

It’s clear vehicles are super important. Driving less is a win-win for us and the Earth. Designing cities better could seriously change things, leading to cleaner air, safer, quieter streets, and a more relaxed feel.

Cars cause a big piece of Europe's greenhouse gases, roughly 12%. But check this out: in some car-dependent spots in the U.S., that can skyrocket to 50%! Basically, cars pollute a ton, mainly in packed cities. One smart cookie suggested future suburbs mirror Vauban's design everywhere, rich or not. More middle-class cars worsen the pollution. Everyone agrees less cars means less smog. Paris limited driving after a crazy smog event, trying to clean things up. Fewer city cars mean less smog and cleaner air for everyone.

Sadly, car crashes are frequent, and people die. You can't stop 'em all, but lots can be avoided. Think of those who die in busy cities or from road rage! One driver nailed it: "Driving stressed me out, now I'm way more chill." Driving is stressful. Less driving, less stress for all. Imagine a nicer place with kids playing and people biking, not horns, engines, and crashes.

Roads eat up so much space. If more people walked, biked, and drove less, we wouldn't need so many roads. We could have bigger sidewalks instead. Some cities are laser-focused on getting folks to drive less, with some “…trying their best to make suburbs smaller…” If shops are clustered on main streets, people can walk, ditching mall drives, boosting safety.

Ultimately, driving less is smart because this cuts pollution and accidents. It also makes our neighborhoods better and quieter. If cities and countries prioritize less driving, the world will be much better. Daily life will also get a whole lot better.

These days, are we maybe leaning on cars way too much?

Remember when Paris said "no" to cars because the smog was just awful, like Source 2 tells us? The air was so bad only certain cars could drive! The smog stuck around for days, almost breaking records! All those cars make smog and hurt our world. But, hey, we can fix this! Driving less has a lot of good stuff.

What if we just didn't need cars? This town in Germany, VAUBAN, did just that! They wanted to see if being car-free made people happier. Getting to work can be a pain with traffic and noise and crashes. Sources 1 and 3 say, "70% of families don't have cars, and 57% sold them when they moved there. 'I was so stressed with a car. I'm happier without it,' said Heidrun Walter." This shows ditching cars might make us feel better.

Cleaner air is another big win when we drive less. When the air got super polluted, Paris banned cars to fight the smog. About 4,000 drivers got tickets, and some even lost their cars for a bit for arguing. What happened? Car traffic in Paris dropped like crazy, 60% in just days. The air got better, so France let more cars back on some days.

Are Americans getting tired of cars? Or are new things making us not need to drive as much? Elisabeth Rosenthal says "the internet lets us work from home and talk online. Cities are getting better, so suburbs aren't as cool. Plus, phones and ride apps make it easy to get around, even vans for work" (source 4, 35). Tech helps us have fewer meetings with apps, calls, texts, and video chats.

Thinking about all of this, I think driving less is good for everyone. I hope you think so too!

Whew, cars are everywhere! Honk, beep, they are always there - in red, blue, and black. Of course, they take us places. But what if suddenly...gone? Could it maybe be...good, saying goodbye to cars? Turns out, yes, it might just make our brains and the Earth happy.

Earth cheers with fewer cars! Cars guzzle gas and then cough out tons of yuck. Think of Europe - cars create 12% of the bad air. But get this: some U.S. cities? A wild 50%! Paris even said "no" to cars in order to beat smog. And guess what? Traffic went way, way down – like, 60% in just five days! Less yuck, less cars, happy Earth. Plus, young people drove less from 2001 to 2009, like 23% less driving! Really smart people think this is going to keep up, meaning even less yuck. The States cause a BIG mess because of cars.

And more good news! Cars gone could make us feel so good, in body and mind. Picture Vauban, a German town, where there are hardly any cars. One person said they used to get stressed about cars, but now they are super happy. Less angry drivers and traffic? Less stress! Bogota, Colombia, has a "Day Without Cars," and everyone's all smiles. One person felt very calm. Better brains, less stress! Here’s something interesting: in Bogota, folks will bike, walk, or bus it that day. Moving around makes you happy! Your brain sends you good feelings. Healthier, happier us, with less cars and more moving.

So, the bottom line is that a happy Earth and happy people say hello, and goodbye to cars! Sure, cars are helpful, but they also cause problems. The next time you grab the keys, maybe think about it. Remember less stress and anger. Remember the fresh air.

Ever stopped to think why the roads become completely jammed, especially around 5 PM each day? The answer is quite straightforward: we depend on cars way too much. Seriously though, less car trips? This is a complete win-win situation and it drastically improves everyone's lives, it’s just a fact.

Sure, cars seem like a fast solution at first glance, but this so-called speed doesn’t come close to making up for the huge amount of negatives. A big downside? Constantly driving turns us into total couch potatoes. A walk or run is great for exercise, but we completely miss out on that when we're always stuck behind the wheel. My friend Walter confessed that owning a car used to stress him out a lot, but he felt so much better after selling it. We really need to cut back on the driving and look for easy and fun ways to travel. Yes, walking might take a bit more time, but it is definitely worth it. Forget the annoying traffic jams, and discover places that cars simply can't reach. Plus, you might find hidden gems, not just the usual roads. Rain can mess with your plans, but Antanas Mockus, a former mayor of Bogota, believed rain shouldn't keep us indoors. Cars do keep us dry, no doubt, but walking is much safer overall. Cities like Germany, Paris, and Bogota want to get rid of cars because of all these problems.

Additionally, less driving could mean fewer instances of road rage. People tend to get very angry with each other while driving, usually because they're too stressed. If we drive less, we'll all be more relaxed. Car accidents are extremely serious, and too many people die from them. Simply put, driving less could actually save lives. Cars cause more problems than they solve. Walking and biking? Definitely easier and safer, for sure. This would also ease congestion, making it simpler for everyone to travel. Drunk driving is terrible and ruins lives. A slightly drunk walker isn't nearly as dangerous as a drunk driver. Seriously, just think about all the bad things about driving too much.

Lastly, Barack Obama pointed out that we must drive less to fight pollution. Reports show fewer Americans buying cars, driving less, and not getting licenses as often, which is great. Paris completely banned cars because of extreme smog, stopping them from entering certain areas. Diesel fuel made the smog problem much worse. In France, 67% of cars use diesel, while only 53% do in other Western European areas. Bogota wants to "make other travel choices more appealing and clean up the air." Those who break the rules get fined. They've learned that driving less reduces stress and makes the air cleaner. Bill Ford envisions cities where "people walking, bikes, cars, trucks, and buses all work together to save time, use less gas, lower pollution, and be safer." These are just some of the many benefits of driving less. Getting rid of cars completely, or just driving less, would make a big difference. Let's get rid of pollution, prevent accidents, and stop underage driving. Just think about all the negative aspects of driving too much versus not driving as much.

Okay, so pollution and global warming are still big issues, right? It's kind of surprising that nobody's totally solved it yet. Cars? Yeah, they're a HUGE part of the problem. What if folks just, you know, drove their cars a whole lot less? Check out some spots that are seriously making changes. Take Vauban, Germany, for example: virtually no cars allowed! And then there's Paris, France; only certain license plates can drive on specific days. Even Bogota, Colombia, is giving it a shot!

Consider Vauban, Germany. There are zero driveways or parking spots here! If you want to park in a garage, be prepared to shell out some serious cash. It turns out that cars contribute to about 12% of Europe's total pollution. It may seem like a small amount, but actually, it's not! If we made more car-free zones, pollution would drop significantly because fewer people would be driving constantly. One resident, Heidrun Walter, mentioned, "When I owned a car, I was always stressed. Now, I'm so much happier!" This indicates that life might be easier without a vehicle. Spending less on gas and maintenance reduces financial anxiety. Costly parking policies could help the environment and save people money.

Now, let's talk about Paris, France. On Mondays, only license plates ending in odd numbers can drive; on Tuesdays, it's even numbers. The pollution levels were even worse than in Beijing! But guess what happened? After putting this system into place, pollution plummeted by a staggering 60% in only FIVE DAYS. Can you believe it? These car regulations really seem to work, and the results are really fast!

Also, Bogota, Colombia, hosts "no car days." Carlos Arturo Plaza stated, "It's fantastic for reducing stress and improving air quality." He clearly sees the connection between cars and their negative impacts. Cali and Valledpar in Colombia are also trying out this initiative! Leaders from other nations are closely watching and expressing their admiration. So, this concept has the potential to spread globally! The result is that people are actually driving less.

The bottom line is that cars are a major problem. However, people rarely consider the pollution they're causing when they're behind the wheel. Some effective solutions include paid parking, implementing odd-even license plate restrictions on specific days, and establishing "no car days."

The United States, this land is for all of us! It's for every person who lives here. We have all the strength, and it's awesome that we pick who leads us. A lot of people think voting for a President and VP is the best thing to do, don't they?

But hold on, there is a hiccup: the Electoral College gets in the way. Sure, electors may be smart and "in the know," but they usually miss the everyday struggles of the average Joe. People know what they live through. As Bradford Plummer says, "the reasons people say it is great are just totally wrong!" (source 14). So, they get Washington, but not what lots of people go through.

Getting involved in how our government works is super important. Think how regular people would feel if the Electoral College was the only one voting—left out and unheard. We have to work together for things to go well. If not, it would be chaos! Richard A. Posner tells us people will "think their vote is nothing, feel like their President doesn't care, and isn't really THEIR leader" (source 19). If they lose NOW, they're angry. What if they can't vote AT ALL?

No way, the Electoral College isn't fair. Richard A. Posner points out some like it because of its "old-fashioned, undemocratic reasons" (source 17). Simple voting works, so why make it complicated? Change might be good, but it's not a guarantee.

Finally, it would be a bad, terrible choice to dump regular voting—unfair and harmful to the country. It would go against "we the people" at its heart.

Okay, even though it’s got issues, many still like the Electoral College. States get votes based on their Congress size (House plus Senate!). You need 270 of the 538 votes to win. Supporters say it picks a clear winner, makes sure all kinds of people get heard, is super important in swing states, helps out bigger states, and means less need for recounts.

Basically, the Electoral College is meant to keep presidential elections safe and fair. It has a lot of power, but it's not perfect. The "winner-takes-all" deal, where the person with the most votes in a state gets all of its electoral votes, seems unfair. Candidates may only bother with big states since they have more electoral votes. Also, you can win the popular vote but still lose the election if you don't get enough electoral votes.

One good thing about keeping the Electoral College is that it helps us get a clear winner. This should stop arguments about the popular vote, as long as electors do what they're supposed to. Also, presidents have to think about what different parts of the country need, since no one area is in charge. People also talk about how it changes things in swing states, giving all votes to the winner. And some say bigger states benefit, so candidates pay more attention to them. This makes a tie less likely, so we don’t have to vote again.

Even though it has benefits, the Electoral College is not perfect. But, some think it's the best way to choose the president. They don't trust just using the popular vote and worry if voters know enough. Despite election rules, those who support the Electoral College think it's better for the country. That’s why it's key to know why some people support it.

Hey Senator, how about a presidential election makeover? Let's ditch the Electoral College and just tally every vote! Shouldn't real people, not electors, choose the leader? Just a thought.

Even heavy hitters like Nixon, Carter, and the Chamber of Commerce wanted it gone. Folks worry electors might go rogue and vote however they feel. Crazy, right?

Remember Obama's win against Romney? Obama dominated the Electoral College, winning by 29 votes. It shows how wonky the system can get, distorting the public's true will. A national vote seems fairer.

Alright, the Electoral College can save the day in close races, like Nixon and Clinton barely winning with 43%. It prevented chaos then. But it still favors something over the people's direct choice. The people should have the ultimate power, I think.

Senator, thanks for considering this. Do we keep the Electoral College or switch to a system where the voters decide? Something to think about.

Dear Senator,

The Electoral College does have some supporters. They say it pushes candidates to pay attention to swing states and connect with smaller towns (Source 3). But frankly, I think that's wrong. I believe it's a really unfair way to pick our President.

Source 2 shows how unfair it is to voters. Because most states give all their votes to the winner, candidates only focus on close races in swing states and ignore everyone else. Seriously, someone could get fewer votes overall but still win the Presidency through the Electoral College. Voters might be blind-sided. This makes you wonder if the winner honestly cares about your state. Source 3 even points out that candidates need to be liked by different kinds of people in different areas to win. A candidate who's popular in just one area, like Rodney in the South, won't bother campaigning hard in those states because they're sure to win anyway. If Mitt Romney ran against Barack Obama, Obama's popularity in the North would majorly affect Romney, and that pretty much sums up the Electoral College.

In the end, I still think the Electoral College is bad and unfair. Source 2 backs me up on this. It argues the Electoral College is unjust, old-fashioned, and doesn't make sense. The reasons for keeping it don't hold up. Basically, the Electoral College goes against what democracy is about and is deeply flawed.

The Electoral College is a head-scratcher, with folks pushing to trash it, saying it's rigged. "Electors," not us, pick the president, based on how we vote. This makes leaders seem out of touch, some say. You can win without the most votes because these electors, who should listen to us, make things messy, so people want it gone.

Plus, most of us don't even know who these electors are. Some states get ignored during campaigns, so it's hard to make good choices. Candidates chase after swing states, hungry for attention and votes. Even though the president is for everyone, this leaves some voters feeling like they don't matter. We're really just voting for electors, and even if you win the popular vote, you might still lose. That's why people want something simpler.

Parties pick "electors" who say they'll back their candidate. The Electoral College champ can lose the popular vote, though it doesn't happen often. Back in 2000, Gore won the vote, but Bush became president—the first time since 1888. It could happen again, changing things even if most people vote differently. The fight's on because most states give all their electoral votes to whoever wins the state. But, it stops elections from dragging on if nobody gets a majority, making sure there's a clear winner. People want their votes to matter, but it doesn't always work out that way.

Lots of people think the Electoral College is a weird way to pick leaders. It was supposed to get people excited about voting, but it can hurt democracy by making votes feel less important. It's super important to make smart choices in a country that loves voting. The system should make sure every single vote counts.

Dear State Senator, I feel strongly that we must keep the Electoral College. It just feels wrong to switch to a popular vote system. The House's role in choosing the president? Super important! It just makes the whole thing feel more fair and organized for everyone. Plus, it helps people understand how we elect our president, which is a great bonus!

One cool thing about the Electoral College is how the House of Representatives might step in. Source One, paragraph three, explains each state gets electors. It's based on its Congressional representation: one for each House member, and two for the Senators. This makes sure tons of people get a voice. Also, Source One, paragraph five, notes, "The candidate's political party usually picks the electors," so it's not just the House. The government has a huge part.

Another awesome part of the Electoral College? It feels more fair! Source Three says, "The Electoral College fixes the problem where big states have too much power because the Senate isn't fair..." So, people trust the presidential election. Source Three also points out, "If everything else is the same, big states get more attention from presidential candidates than small states..." The Electoral College really helps the bigger states.

Most importantly, the Electoral College keeps us safe from election problems, which is the big thing for me. Source Three, paragraph twenty-two, explains, "The Electoral College stops problems in elections where no candidate gets the most votes." This makes voting simple. That same source and paragraph also say, "We don't need more elections when no one wins the most votes; the Electoral College makes sure there's always a clear winner..." A relief, indeed!

Look, some people might dislike the Electoral College if their candidate loses, but that's no reason to get rid of it. It keeps presidential elections in order. Source Two, paragraph Ten, says, "With the Electoral College, we don't vote directly for the president, but for electors, who then choose the president." Some might find it unfair. But electors vote for their state's pick, which is fair enough. Source Two also notes, "Basically, the Electoral College is unfair. Because of the winner-takes-all system, candidates only care about close races in 'swing' states." Even with these issues, the Electoral College does much more than count votes; it helps our government pick the best president, like I said!

Basically, I'm all for keeping the Electoral College! It's good for citizens and the government. It makes elections fair, stops chaos, and cuts down on problems, so people are happier with the result. Each year, the Electoral College makes elections easier to get and more organized. Even though the government is super involved, individual votes still count big time. They are key to the Electoral College, and so are government votes.

Does the Electoral College even matter anymore? It helped long ago when states struggled to count votes. People voted by state, and that was acceptable. But now, with better tech, it feels slow and unfair. If you're a Republican in a heavily Democratic state, your vote seems meaningless. Wouldn't a national vote where everyone's voice counts be better? Plus, some states have too much power due to the winner-takes-all system. Small states don't get enough attention, making it hard to decide who to vote for.

Back in the day, when the telegraph was brand new, things were different! Instead of states counting votes and sending people, they needed another way. Imagine getting votes by horse! Too risky! Papers could be lost, people could get hurt, and some places might not get counted. The Electoral College made sense then. But like the telegraph, it's outdated. Now, phones can hold many votes. Computers can quickly do math, giving us fast answers. We can send votes quickly and fix mistakes. What if a computer counted everything? Each place sends info to one computer, with checks and balances. Use multiple computers to prevent cheating. It's a shame some people won't let go of old ways! So outdated and ineffective!

It's just not right. A Democrat votes in a strong Republican area, but the Republican always wins. That Democrat's vote is as if it never existed. Gone. Every time. But a national vote would make those votes count. They would matter! The winner-takes-all system makes it worse. Winning California by a bit means getting ALL their votes, ignoring what half the people think! Unfair. Big states dominate due to more votes. What about the small states? Not much attention. Politicians prefer big states, ignoring the small ones. Voters in places like the Dakotas might not know much and just pick a party. The main battles are in big states. The ads aren't always true, but voters figure it out. Swing states get lots of info. Small states? Forgotten, so voters don't know as much, and their votes might not matter. A national vote means candidates go everywhere, no more big-state bias, and fair voting for all.

So, here's the deal: The Electoral College was fine back then, but now? It's old and unfair. We can easily talk and travel, making it pointless and bad for our democracy. It's time for a change!

Across the world, folks are ditching their cars more and more often. This is great for nature and your wallet. Lots of people want to do their part, even if it's just in their own town. Countries like China, Spain, and the U.S. are seeing fewer people using cars these days. Some might not like it, but others are happy because of all the yucky pollution. Just think, if everyone cut their driving in half, the air would be so much cleaner! Sure, cars are handy, but they cause a whole bunch of problems.

Check out Vauban, Germany, where you barely see any cars. You can own a car, but parking is a real pain and super expensive. It could cost you up to $40,000 just to park near your own place! So, most people park their cars outside the area. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote a piece called "Life Goes On Without Cars in German Suburb," saying that about 70% of families in Vauban don't even own a car. Plus, over half the people who moved there sold their cars! Cutting back on driving can have good and bad sides. But, you can save a lot of money on car payments, parking tickets, and pricey gas. And hey, the air gets cleaner too.

Paris, France, had some serious pollution problems, so they had to ban some cars. Robert Duffer said that cars with license plates ending in even numbers couldn't drive for a bit. If you did, it was a €22 fine. The next day, they banned odd-numbered plates. This really helped cut down on pollution! That's awesome, especially for a big city. The main idea was to make the air better so people could breathe easier. Duffer pointed out that "4,000 drivers got fined" and they "impounded 27 cars." Traffic went down by 60%! Once the air got better, they lifted the ban.

Bogota, Columbia, started with a small thing that blew up big time. Andrew Selsky wrote that "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota." It started small, but now everyone loves it.

Once a year, no one in the city can use their cars! You can still use buses and taxis, though. Around seven million people are trying to make the air better. The goal is to have cleaner ways to get around with way less pollution. If you break the rules, you get a fine! Selsky mentions that Bogota has "118 MILES of bicycle paths!" Parks are nicer, sports are greener, and the air is fresher. It's so popular that other cities are doing it too.

Rosenthal also talked about the U.S. She said that "Recent studies suggest that Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less and getting fewer licenses." Rosenthal 29. This lines up with "Obama's ambitious goals to curb the United States' greenhouse gas emissions!" Rosenthal 29. In New York City, you can easily grab a taxi or hop on the subway. Parking is a nightmare, so people don't want to own cars. Think of all the cash you'd save! Traffic is still crazy, but imagine how much worse it would be with even more cars. It shows how much the city depends on public transit. But public transport can be pricey, and without it, the air would be terrible.

So, cutting back on car use has tons of perks. It fights pollution, makes the air cleaner, and saves you money. If you can, walking is a great choice for your health, your bank account, and the planet. Sure, driving can be faster sometimes, but think about the costs and the pollution. Try using your car less and see the good stuff that happens.

Car sales? Sky-high! Companies? Raking it in! But hold up, should we maybe pump the brakes on all this driving? Honestly, study after study shows too much time behind the wheel is a stress factory. As Carlos Arturo Plaza pointed out in Andrew Selsky's "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," less driving equals less stress for everyone. Good for you, GREAT for the planet! It's weird, right? Tons of Americans drive, but fewer are actually buying cars. Maybe carpooling's on their minds? Who knows! Yeah, the bus might be a bit slow, but hey, it's a solid option! Seriously, cutting down on driving could lead to some seriously awesome changes.

Cars? Major polluters! They're wrecking the air and the ozone layer, big time. Sure, cars make life easy, and that license at 16? Sweet! But we always forget how much owning a car can suck. Like, always needing gas! Imagine if we drove less - HUGE difference! Less driving helps the ozone, and we can actually breathe again. Robert Duffer's "Paris bans driving due to smog" shows how awesome driving bans can be. Paris? Super smoggy! They banned driving to clean things up. Cold nights plus warm days? Traps all those nasty car fumes, making the smog worse, which caused the ban. The air in Paris got way better after that. The article mentioned, "Paris typically has more smog than other European capitals... Last week Paris had 147 micrograms of particulate matter PM per cubic meter compared with the 114 in Brussels and 79.7 in London..."

Okay, nobody's saying to junk your car, but think about the downsides. Maybe you'll realize it's a headache. Vauban, Germany gets it! They've totally changed how they live. Elizabeth Rosenthal's "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," tells how the town lives car-free. People there say they are happier now, no traffic or crashes! They ditched street parking, driveways, and garages. Who needs 'em without cars? Instead, Vauban made sidewalks and walking better. One resident, a media trainer with kids, said, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Before, the government spent 80 percent on highways, and only 20 percent on everything else. But in the next six years, things are changing.

So, we gotta rethink how we move around in cities. Less driving? Awesome perks! Government can use money better, air gets cleaner, we feel better, and we save cash. Seriously, is all that driving REALLY worth the trouble? We need to ask ourselves that, for real.

U.S. roads? Packed! Though folks love zooming, few dig walking. The rub? Everyone drives, causing jams, which makes trips longer! Cars hurt the earth too, making the air nasty. Less cars? Great! Good for us and the planet.

Less car use? Awesome! It helps Earth and makes us feel good. Get this: a study said, "Cars cause 12% of Europe's smog...but in the U.S., it's sometimes 50%!" Since cars stink, carpool or bus it! Plus, walk or bike? Good for your health!

Smog, that ugly haze over cities, comes from car fumes. It's full of bad stuff that makes folks sick and asthma worse, especially in cities. Like Beijing, China – known for smog. "Paris even banned cars to clean the air." Yep, Paris fixed it. Less cars? Clean air!

But wait, there's more! Cars mess with water and land too. They use gas from the ground. Getting gas? It wrecks nature, like oil spills that kill sea life. So, take trains, buses, or walk/bike! Less gas used that way. Since cars guzzle gas, less cars means way less gas used overall.

Folks say life's easier and calmer without a car. No more jams or crashes! During a "Day Without Cars" in Bogota, Colombia, Carlos Arturo Plaza cheered, "'Less stress and cleaner air, yay!'" See? Less cars, less stress. Heidrun Walter said, "'I stressed with a car. Now I'm happier!'" She's in a German town where cars matter less. Less stress? Another reason to ditch the car for good.

Some folks need a car when buses and trains won't work. As someone said, "They walk, bus, or share cars," and "'A car is just for tough trips.'" By not driving when we don't need to, we can make a better system where we don't need cars as much, and other ways to get around are better. Better buses? Less car use helps Earth and us.

Less cars? Good for the Earth and relaxing. One source said, "Cars are the second biggest cause of smog in the U.S.!" If we don't drive and take buses, we can cut smog a lot in America. Less smog, healthier folks, less stress? All thanks to fewer cars!

So, ditching the car? Actually a great idea! Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote about a German town called Vauban in her article. It's the kind of place where you barely see any cars. A woman who lives there, Heidrun Walter, says life is way better without them. Instead of loud cars, you hear happy kids and bikes going by! The streets are busy, calm, and really clean.

Here's the deal: less driving could really help our suburbs. Rosenthal's article shows that cities have been getting more crowded and easier to walk around for twenty years! The thought? Smaller suburbs that are simple to get around in, with good buses and trains, and stores nearby – so we don't need cars so much. Even the EPA likes this idea a lot!

There's a big reward: cleaner air when we don't drive. Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," talks about how Paris tried to fix pollution by stopping some cars from driving. And guess what? It worked! Traffic went down a lot – 60% – after just five smoggy days. Before, the pollution was as bad as Beijing, China, which is known for having awful air. Paris showed that less driving means less pollution and traffic.

Also, there's Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in bogota." It talks about Bogota's car-free day, which encourages people to use other ways to get around and lower smog. The result? New parks, sports areas, better sidewalks, restaurants, and nice shops. A business owner there, Carlos Arturo Plaza, now finds biking with his wife easier and better for the environment.

Basically? Less driving is good for everyone! Less smog, less pollution, and easier to get to stores. It makes our lives better now and helps save the earth for the future.

Cars, a true innovation, gave birth to the modern automobile. Nowadays, factories churn out countless cars each year, significantly improving how we get around, no matter the distance. However, these gas-powered vehicles release a lot of CO2, harming the Earth with ozone layer damage and acid rain that eats away at things. Plus, using cars too much leads to traffic jams and other headaches. Owning a car is also really expensive. To protect our planet, we need to build and drive fewer cars.

Indeed, cars cause a lot of pollution by releasing CO2, which speeds up global warming. Like it says in Article 1, paragraph 5, "Cars cause 12% of greenhouse gases in Europe... but in the U.S., it might be as high as 50%!" If we made and used fewer cars, the environment would be better off. Slowing down global warming means we can save places like the Arctic and animals like penguins and polar bears.

Traffic jams pop up all the time without warning. As noted in Passage 2, paragraph 14, "Paris's traffic was cut by 60%" by only allowing certain license plates on the road on certain days. Easier travel would be great! Imagine a long three-hour delay turning into a quick twenty-minute trip, giving people more free time. Less driving and traffic also means fewer accidents.

Car costs, whether you're buying or leasing, quickly empty your bank account. From the start, you're in debt, and it affects everyone. Article 4, paragraph 32, mentions that "Poor Americans couldn't afford cars, and people without jobs weren't driving to work." Spending too much on cars hurts people's wallets and the whole economy. Less car debt would make things better for everyone's finances.

In short, there are many good reasons to cut back on car use. Cars hurt the planet through melting ice, ozone damage, and worse weather. Less traffic makes it easier to get around and frees up your time. Making and leasing fewer cars could help the economy. Basically, less car use is good for the planet and everyone who lives here.

Okay, so the Electoral College, a really old thing, makes lots of folks mad. Instead of directly voting for the president, we pick "electors." Here’s the wild part: these electors could ditch who you wanted! Remember back in 2000? The Electoral College put someone in charge even though they didn't win the popular vote! Some say it stops ties, but that doesn't really make sense.

This old way of doing things is a problem, especially like what happened in 2000. That election was a mess! Getting rid of it could stop that from happening again. Also, back in 1960, some people who wanted to keep segregation almost got the Democratic electors in Louisiana to vote against Kennedy. Electors can change their minds, which is awful. It doesn't happen often, but it's a *huge* deal.

The Electoral College is not just weird, it's super unfair. Every state gets a vote, so someone in Wyoming, where not many people live, has as much power as tons of people in California! They say it stops ties, but what about 1979? Just a few votes in Ohio could have made things a tie! Because of the "winner-take-all" thing, candidates ignore some states. Some states didn't even get looked at in 2000, so people didn't even get a chance to vote.

A lot of people feel left out, especially after Al Gore won the popular vote but still lost in 2000. Voters might not even bother voting if they think their state is already decided, like Democrats in Texas or Republicans in California. This isn't a joke; their vote *matters*. A popular vote could cause ties, but it's still better than this messed-up system. Over 60 percent of people hate the Electoral College – that's *a lot*! We need to change things before it gets worse.

It’s really clear: there have been mistakes, close calls, problems, and unfairness. People who defend the Electoral College are wrong. What happened in 2000 and how angry everyone is shows how crazy it is to keep this around. The system is still here, probably for a while. But if more people know about it, we can start to fix things.

So, you're leaving the car at home more? Fantastic! Cleaner air is a huge win, plus, you discover awesome new ways to travel. And guess what? The streets suddenly feel much safer.

Fewer cars mean less pollution. It's great for us and the planet. Vauban, Germany, is rocking this! Cars cause so many greenhouse gases—around 12% of Europe's total. Nuts, huh? And get this: the U.S. is way worse, at 50%! Cities like Vauban, Paris, and Bogota are trying to cut car use and clean up the air. Experts think this car-free thing is here to stay, and that's massive for cutting carbon. It turns out, transport is the second biggest polluter in the U.S. Plus, when you ditch the car, you find cooler ways to get around. Forget traffic, wander through parks, play ball, just move! Bogota, Colombia, tried a car-free day. People walked, biked, skated, or used the bus. The streets were wide open! Paris even banned some cars to clean up, which cut traffic by 60%. They made public transport free for a long weekend, which just shows how less driving helps everyone.

Heidrun Walter, who doesn't own a car, says she's much happier without one. Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, says driving less makes him calmer and helps the environment. Also, less driving means less risk of accidents. M. Sivak's research shows fewer young adults (16 to 39) are even getting their driver's licenses, while older folks keep driving. One study saw a 23% drop in driving among young adults between 2001 and 2009. This switch is a major win for everyone. The number of miles driven is almost 9% below its highest point, and fewer people own cars now than before the recession. Because of all this, people might not go back to relying on cars as much anytime soon.

In short, driving less is amazing for helping the planet, finding new ways to travel, and making communities safer.

Ugh, traffic, am I right? What a headache, seriously! Whether you're behind the wheel or not, it messes with everyone. Remember when cars were like, *the* way to go? Well, not so much anymore. With all the gunk they spew, fewer folks are driving as much, ya know? Less driving would be awesome – less stress, cleaner air, and just feeling good, period!

Picture this: Way less stress without those awful traffic jams and always rushing. We could hop on bikes, take the bus or train, or just stroll around! Look at Vauban, Germany. Turns out, it’s mostly car-free, and people there seem way chiller 'cause they can walk everywhere, easy peasy!

And get this: Cars are terrible for the environment, like, *really* bad. They pollute the air like crazy. In Europe, cars cause about 12% of global warming emissions! In the US? Some areas with a ton of cars crank out almost 50%! Paris is a prime example of how awful cars can be. One study showed that the air got as gross as Beijing's! They even banned cars for a bit to clean things up.

Fewer cars help both us and the planet. Check this out: Bogota, Colombia, has a car-free day every year. It makes the air way better, and people dig it! Even when it pours, they show up for biking events. It worked so well that two other cities in Colombia are giving it a shot. Bogota even has the longest bike path network in Latin America, about 118 miles long! Aside from what the government's doing, people are driving less on their own too. Reports say driving is down by about 9%. Maybe it’s 'cause more folks are working from home, so they don’t need to drive as much.

So, things are changing, maybe for the better. Cars are losing their grip, which means cleaner air, less stress, and a happier life for us all!

In Britain, the party with the most seats picks their leader, but when we vote, we ALL get a voice! That’s the big difference between direct democracy and what Britain does. Leaders can be convincing, but not always good, so stopping them from abusing power is a MUST when we vote. To be fair and keep power with the people, the U.S. needs to stick with the Electoral College.

Because our founders were so smart, the U.S. has been a republic for a long time. The Electoral College came from both Britain AND the Iroquois, although some folks don't see the Iroquois influence. British politicians pick the Prime Minister, and U.S. founders also wanted a way to elect a leader, worried a directly elected President could become a dictator. So, the President had to listen to Congress, and Congress could boot out a bad President. Think of the Electoral College as stopping dictators. Look at Hitler: never elected. Mussolini got popular. The Electoral College stops this with regular people; it's not perfect, but it represents us. Richard A. Posner said it's undemocratic because electors, not us, vote. All governments have problems, sadly. Some folks want to ditch the Electoral College, even though it was created with good intentions. Even Nixon, who quit in scandal, wanted to get rid of it. Critics like Bradford Plumer think it's old and unfair. But, the Electoral College keeps elections safe, and electors for each state keep things fair for everyone. That's voting that represents us!

The Electoral College represents us all; it was made thoughtfully by America's founders. Even with disagreements, great presidents like Kennedy, Roosevelt, Lincoln, Washington, and Jefferson were elected because of it. To stop tyranny and give power to the people, the U.S. HAS to keep the Electoral College.

So, Senator, the Electoral College thing? Seriously old-school, yeah? Why can't we just directly vote for the President? Feels super rigged, honestly, like the whole thing's broken. We're already stressing about everything, we need fair play, especially with this debt situation. Obama didn't exactly solve it, did he? Where you happen to live shouldn't give your vote extra oomph.

And these electors? Way too much juice! Makes our votes feel kinda useless. Sure, we "pick" them, but they're the ones who actually decide. That's a major trust issue for the government. Shouldn't the actual voters have a say about what happens with the Electoral College? Bet tons of people agree. Okay, I'm young, but I still have a voice, you know?

Plumer's spot on: we're not really voting direct. Remember the whole 2000 mess? Gore got more votes but still lost. If we're all about equality, why do electors get special treatment? It makes our votes feel cheap. If electors decide, why even bother voting in the first place?

People claim the Electoral College stops one group from steamrolling everyone else. But, the winner-takes-all thing decides everything anyway. So, seriously, who *are* these electors and why are they so special? I'm young, but I'm not an idiot. Why isn't anyone listening to me? Feels like America's built on a lie, not fair play. We think we're in control, but really it's all just politicians and the Electoral College pulling all the strings.

America's going downhill if we don't make changes. Getting rid of the Electoral College? Huge step in the right direction. Why should you even care about what I think? I'm just a kid. But I know what it feels like to be ignored, even when I'm right. America knows what needs to happen. Don't let a tiny few decide everything for us. Let *us* pick the president, even if it's just a test run. If it's a total disaster, fine, we can go back to the old way. But we have to try it out to know for sure.

Alright, listen up: Most of us just take cars for granted, right? But check it – they're actually a pretty big deal. Sure, we see 'em everywhere, but a ton of people can't actually afford them or even drive. Take Vauban in Germany, for instance. They straight up BANNED cars! They've got a train that goes to Freiburg and roads on the outside, but no cars allowed inside the city. You can own a car, but parking? Forget about it. Almost zero spots, and they cost a fortune. A garage way out of town? It'll run you forty grand EXTRA! Get this: Seventy percent of the people living there got rid of their cars. And get this, over half SOLD their cars after moving there! Heidrun Walter, a media trainer and mother, hit the nail on the head when she said, "I was so stressed out when I had a car. This is SO much better." Since '06, Vauban is showing us a glimpse into the future: Car-free suburbs. Pretty rad, don't you think?

Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote this article called "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," and she totally nailed it. Cars are cool and all, but HEY, they give you AIR POLLUTION! They're seriously messing up the planet! Paris got so smoggy that they banned cars for a little bit. One Monday? Even license plates had to stay home or cough up some dough. The next day, maybe it was the odd numbers? The air got so bad because the weather trapped all the car fumes. Crazy, right? Traffic dropped like crazy – like sixty percent in only five days! The air quality was like Beijing. Banning half the cars cleared the air like magic! The odd numbered cars drove the next day.

Bogota, Colombia, threw a NO-CAR DAY. Walk, ride your bike, skate, hop on a bus – whatever floats your boat! Just NO cars, that's it. And you know what? ZERO ACCIDENTS! They've been doing it every single year! And this was their third year! They thought around seven million people would have to find another way to get around. It rained, but who even cares? People STILL showed up! Mayor Antanas Mockus said, "Rain didn't stop anybody!" Everyone was super relaxed, and the air was way cleaner, too. Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, biked alongside his wife. "It's great for stress, and good for pollution," he said.

So, here in the US, cars are HUGE. But are we over them yet? Some folks think so. We're a big country with lots of spread-out suburbs, so we've always been obsessed with cars. Model T, Detroit, "Mustang Sally" – that's American as apple pie. But maybe things are starting to change. By 2013, driving was down almost eight percent. That's back to '95 levels! Money was tight, and fewer people were driving to work. But Michael Sivak from Michigan made a cool point. "Car ownership dropped BEFORE the economy even got bad." Wow. We need some other options for getting around so we can chill on this car craze. New York's bike rentals, tolls, car rentals? Good first steps. One study even showed that young people drove twenty-three percent LESS between 2001 and 2009!

Folks are starting to want fewer cars because they want to be safer, save some cash, and help the earth.

Every single person seems to be driving constantly these days, whether it's for commuting to their jobs, attending classes, or participating in extracurricular activities. While it may seem convenient initially, it can quickly escalate into a significant issue. Although altering this habit may appear challenging, doing so can yield substantial benefits. The air quality improves, our overall health is enhanced, and we experience increased happiness. It's due to these reasons that leaders are actively seeking ways to diminish our reliance on driving.

According to Robert Duffer, Beijing experienced severe air pollution in 2014 largely due to automobile emissions (source 2, paragraph 14). In a similar vein, Paris implemented restrictions on driving during certain days for individuals whose license plates ended with specific numbers, imposing fines for non-compliance (source 2, paragraphs 10-19). Upon observing improvements in air quality, they ceased enforcing the rule. This demonstrates the impactful difference that reducing driving can make! Elizabeth Rosenthal noted in 2009 that cars contribute to the deterioration of air quality in both Europe and the United States (source 1, paragraph 5), meaning driving less results in cleaner air and reduced gasoline consumption.

Although commuting to work by car may appear convenient, envision the heightened sense of well-being you'd experience by reducing your driving frequency. Rosenthal cited Vauban, Germany, where many families opt not to own a car (source 1, paragraph 3). Heidrum Walter, a resident of Vauban without a vehicle, expresses feeling less stressed as a result (source 1, paragraph 3). Andrew Selsky recounted how cars were prohibited in Bogota, Columbia, for a single day in 2002 (source 3, paragraph 21). Carlos Arturo Plaza, residing in Bogota, noted feeling considerably calmer and experiencing improved air quality during this time. Clearly, diminishing our driving habits enhances our overall well-being, a change we should embrace for ourselves!

The notion of reducing driving is widely embraced due to its numerous advantages. The EPA actively supports towns aiming to decrease car usage, with leaders also lending their support (source 1, paragraph 9). David Goldberg argues that post-World War II designs have fostered excessive car dependency, necessitating adjustments. Mimi Sheller observes that transformations are already underway, suggesting it's only a matter of time (source 4, paragraph 35). Consensus appears to be that reducing driving is generally beneficial. Let's commit to driving less frequently, both for our own well-being and for the planet's sake!

Significant shifts are unfolding globally! The younger demographic is driving less compared to previous generations (source 4, paragraph 41). This juncture presents an opportune moment to opt for buses, bicycles, or trains instead of cars. Reduced driving leads to cleaner air and improved overall health. The EPA advocates for less driving for these compelling reasons, urging us to disseminate this awareness worldwide. This action is essential for safeguarding both the environment and future generations. Reducing driving is truly advantageous for the world; therefore, let's actively pursue this change!

Senator, allow me to offer my perspective. I'm just a student, admittedly not a political expert. However, I firmly believe the Electoral College is the correct approach. Doesn't it streamline the presidential election process?

The Electoral College comprises 538 electors; securing 270 votes guarantees victory. While we technically vote for electors, our votes truly reflect our support for a candidate and their vision. Our current system elevates the importance of swing states, a major benefit. As "In Defense of the Electoral College" argues, it enhances state engagement. This potentially prevents prolonged elections and safeguards the interests of smaller states.

Many states employ the "winner-take-all" rule, allocating all electoral votes to the state's popular vote winner. Alternative methods like "proportional representation" exist, yet "winner-take-all" holds greater appeal. While the Electoral College can appear complex, this aspect simplifies matters. Unanimously allocating a state's votes to one candidate fosters stability, unlike proportional representation, which could delay election outcomes.

Essentially, the Electoral College boasts a long history. "What Is the Electoral College?" details its establishment by the founders to achieve equilibrium. They sought to avoid presidential selection by Congress or direct popular vote. Thus, understanding its historical context is crucial. Thank you for considering my views.

Senator, can I speak frankly?

Why not get rid of the Electoral College and just use the popular vote to decide things? Whoever gets the most votes should win, that's all there is to it! It seems like the fairest way to pick our leader, don't you agree? This whole elector business—voting for electors who *then* choose the president—feels a bit shady, if you want my honest opinion. Shouldn't everyone get a say in who's in charge, not just the "big shots"? It's time to revamp our voting system.

Seriously, what's the point of electors anyway? "The Indefensible Electoral College" (source 2) hits the nail on the head—the system is totally rigged! Candidates just set up shop in swing states. But then, "In Defense of the Electoral College" (source 3) argues that no single area can win by itself. However, the Electoral College basically ignores some places; their votes don't even matter, so the president can simply disregard them.

And one more thing, *everybody* should be able to vote, not just citizens. Leaving people out just because they aren't citizens? That's completely unfair. Some areas consistently get the short end of the stick with electors, which makes certain voices louder than others.
